title: Information Exposition
text:
Preface
Welcome to Information Exposition, a hands-on guide to communicating the findings of data analyses using Python. This book grew out of an undergraduate course at the University of Colorado Boulder designed for information science majors who have completed one or two semesters of programming and want to develop both their computational and communication skills for working with data.

title: Information Exposition
text:
Who This Book Is For
This book is for you if you have some experience writing Python code and want to learn how to load, reshape, visualize, and communicate data effectively. You should be comfortable with basic Python syntax — variables, loops, functions, and importing libraries — but you do not need prior experience with pandas, matplotlib, or statistical modeling. We will build those skills together, chapter by chapter.
If you have taken an introductory data science or statistics course, you will find that this book extends those foundations with a strong emphasis on communication: not just producing a correct chart, but producing a chart that tells a clear and honest story to a real audience.

title: Information Exposition
text:
How This Book Is Organized
The book is organized into four parts, each corresponding to a module that pairs computational skills with communication skills.
Part I: Shaping Data introduces the foundational pandas operations you will use throughout the rest of the book. You will learn to load data from CSV files, summarize data with groupby-aggregation, combine multiple datasets with concatenation and merging, and reshape data between wide and long formats. The part concludes with a chapter on designing clear and effective data visualizations.
Part II: Distributions covers visualizing the shape of data. You will learn to build histograms with matplotlib’s object-oriented interface, compare distributions across categories with seaborn’s categorical plots, and think carefully about who your audience is and what they need from your analysis.
Part III: Trends focuses on temporal data. You will work with Python’s datetime library and pandas’s Timestamp objects, visualize time series with rolling averages and resampling, and grapple with the challenge of communicating uncertainty to audiences who crave certainty.
Part IV: Relationships explores bivariate and multivariate relationships. You will build scatterplots, estimate and interpret linear regression models, and develop the critical thinking skills to evaluate whether a relationship is causal or merely correlational.

title: Information Exposition
text:
Recurring Themes
Several themes recur across chapters and are worth flagging at the outset.
Verify your data constantly. Every chapter reinforces the habit of checking the shape, size, and contents of your data after every operation. Does the number of rows match your expectation? Are there unexpected null values? Did a merge introduce duplicates? These checks are not busywork — they are the difference between trustworthy analysis and garbage.
Visualization is iterative. You will rarely produce a publication-ready chart on the first try. The chapters model an iterative process: start with a default plot, identify what is unclear or misleading, and refine through customization of labels, scales, colors, and annotations.
Communication is as important as computation. A correct analysis that no one can understand is not useful. Throughout the book, we pay attention to writing, audience, narrative structure, and the ethical responsibilities that come with putting data stories into the world.

title: Information Exposition
text:
Conventions
Code cells appear with syntax highlighting and are ready to run in a Jupyter notebook or Quarto document, provided you have the datasets and libraries installed. Because the datasets are hosted separately from this book, all code cells are shown but not executed during the book’s build process.






TipTips



Green callout boxes like this one offer practical advice, shortcuts, or best practices.








WarningWarnings



Yellow callout boxes highlight common mistakes or pitfalls to avoid.








NoteExercises





Blue collapsible callout boxes contain exercises with solutions. Try the exercise yourself before expanding the solution.

title: Information Exposition
text:
Accompanying Materials
All datasets used in this book are available in the companion GitHub repository at https://github.com/cuinfoscience/Information-Exposition-Book/. See 15  Datasets for a description of each dataset.
The Python environment setup instructions, including how to install Anaconda, Jupyter, and the required libraries, are provided in 16  Python Environment Setup.

title: Information Exposition
text:
Acknowledgments
This book was developed for INFO 3402: Information Exposition in the Department of Information Science at the University of Colorado Boulder. It draws on the work of many excellent textbooks and resources in data visualization and communication, including Jonathan Schwabish’s Better Data Visualizations, Claus Wilke’s Fundamentals of Data Visualization, and the pandas, matplotlib, and seaborn documentation communities.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.1 Setup
Every data analysis begins with the same mundane but critical step: getting data into your computing environment. This chapter introduces pandas’s read_csv function and the many parameters that give you fine-grained control over how raw files become usable DataFrames. By the end of this chapter, you will be able to load a CSV file, inspect its contents, handle messy headers and missing values, filter columns, sort rows, and compute new columns from existing ones.
These skills may feel mechanical, but they form the foundation for everything that follows. A DataFrame that is loaded incorrectly — with the wrong header row, unrecognized missing values, or columns stored as the wrong data type — will silently corrupt every analysis built on top of it.
We begin by importing the libraries we will use throughout the book. If any of these imports fail, see Chapter 16 for installation instructions.
# Embed visualization outputs in the notebook
%matplotlib inline

# Import matplotlib's pyplot
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Import numpy
import numpy as np

# Import pandas
import pandas as pd

# Customize pandas to display more columns than the default
pd.options.display.max_columns = 500
A quick sanity check: does your environment execute code correctly?
1 + 1
print("{0} has the best haircut of any INFO faculty!".format('Brian Keegan'))
If those cells ran without errors, you are ready to work with data.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.2 Loading a CSV File
The workhorse function for reading tabular data in pandas is read_csv. At its simplest, you pass it a file path and it returns a DataFrame. We will work with data from the U.S. Census Bureau’s American Community Survey (ACS), specifically the DP05 table of demographic and housing estimates by state. This dataset is included in the book’s repository as ACS2019_DP05_state.csv. See Chapter 15 for provenance details.

# From https://data.census.gov
census_df = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv')

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.3 Inspecting the Data
Before doing anything else, look at the data. The .head() method shows the first five rows by default. Use .tail() for the last five, and .shape to see the number of rows and columns as a tuple.

census_df.head()


census_df.shape







TipBuild the habit early



After every operation that changes a DataFrame — loading, filtering, merging, grouping — check .shape and .head(). This takes seconds and catches problems before they compound.


You should notice something odd right away: the column names may not be what you expect. Census data files often include a metadata row at the top that read_csv interprets as column names, pushing the actual column names down into the first row of data. Let’s look at that first row to confirm.

census_df.loc[0]


1.3.1 Understanding the Census documentation
The Census Bureau’s documentation explains several special codes that appear throughout the data:

An ** entry in the margin of error column indicates too few sample observations to compute a standard error.
A - entry in the estimate column indicates too few observations, or that a ratio of medians cannot be calculated.
A + following a median estimate means the median falls in the upper interval of an open-ended distribution.
A *** entry indicates the median falls in the lowest or upper interval of an open-ended distribution.
A ***** entry indicates the estimate is controlled and a sampling variability test is not appropriate.
An N entry indicates the number of sample cases is too small.
An (X) means the estimate is not applicable or not available.

That is a lot of different ways to say “missing.” We will need to handle all of them.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.4 Fixing Headers with read_csv Parameters
Pandas’s read_csv function has dozens of parameters documented in the User Guide for CSV files and the read_csv API reference. We will use several of the most important ones throughout this chapter.

1.4.1 Strategy 1: Tell read_csv which row to use
The header parameter tells pandas which row (or rows) to use as column names. If the real column names are in the second row (index 1), pass header=[1].

dp05_df = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv', header=[1])

dp05_df.head()



1.4.2 Strategy 2: Fix headers after loading
Alternatively, you can reassign the .columns attribute using the values in the misplaced row, then drop that row.

census_df.columns = census_df.loc[0]
census_df = census_df.drop(index=0)
census_df.head()

Both strategies work. The first is generally preferable because it handles the problem at the point of loading, reducing the chance that you forget to clean it up later. Throughout this book, we will favor making read_csv do the work whenever possible.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.5 Handling Missing Values
The na_values parameter accepts a list of strings that read_csv should treat as NaN (Not a Number, pandas’s standard missing value indicator). Given the Census documentation above, we need to handle several codes simultaneously.

1.5.1 Strategy 1: Tell read_csv to handle it

dp05_df = pd.read_csv(
    'ACS2019_DP05_state.csv',
    header=[1],
    na_values=['(X)', '*****', '-', '+', '***']
)

dp05_df.head()



1.5.2 Strategy 2: Replace values after loading
If you have already loaded the data, you can use .replace() to swap specific values for NaN.

dp05_df = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv', header=[1])

# Option A: replace inplace
dp05_df.replace({'*****': np.nan, '(X)': np.nan}, inplace=True)

# Option B: overwrite the variable (equivalent)
# dp05_df = dp05_df.replace({'*****': np.nan, '(X)': np.nan})



1.5.3 Strategy 3: Drop the offending columns entirely
In some cases, if entire columns are full of missing values, you may decide to drop them rather than impute. This is a valid choice, but be aware of what information you lose.






WarningDon’t ignore missing value codes



If you skip this step, columns that should be numeric will be stored as strings because of stray characters like (X). Numeric operations like .sum() and .mean() will fail silently or raise confusing errors downstream.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.6 Filtering Columns
The ACS reports each variable as a triplet: an estimate, a margin of error, and a percentage. If you only need the estimates, you can filter columns during loading or after.

1.6.1 Strategy 1: Use usecols during loading
The usecols parameter accepts a function that is called on each column name. If the function returns True, the column is included.

dp05_df = pd.read_csv(
    'ACS2019_DP05_state.csv',
    header=1,
    na_values=['(X)', '*****', '-', '+', '***'],
    usecols=lambda col: 'Estimate' in col or 'Geographic Area Name' in col
)

dp05_df.head()



1.6.2 Strategy 2: Filter columns after loading
You can iterate over .columns and select those that match your criteria, then subset the DataFrame.

estimate_columns = []

for col in dp05_df.columns:
    if 'Estimate' in col:
        estimate_columns.append(col)

estimate_columns += ['Geographic Area Name']

# Give me all the rows, but only the columns in estimate_columns
dp05_estimates_df = dp05_df.loc[:, estimate_columns]







TipList comprehensions



The loop above can be written more concisely as a list comprehension:
estimate_columns = [col for col in dp05_df.columns if 'Estimate' in col]
List comprehensions are a Pythonic pattern you will see frequently. They are shorter and often faster than equivalent for loops.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.7 Sorting
The .sort_values() method sorts a DataFrame by one or more columns. To find the states with the largest Asian population:

cols = ['Geographic Area Name', 'Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian']

dp05_df.sort_values(
    by='Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian',
    ascending=False
).loc[:, cols]

California, New York, and Texas top the list — but these are also the three most populous states. Is the Asian population large relative to the total population, or is it simply large because the state is large?

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.8 Computing New Columns
States with large Asian populations may simply have large total populations. We lost the percentages when we filtered to only “Estimate” columns in the step above. Let’s compute our own percentage. You create a new column by assigning the result of an arithmetic operation between existing columns.

num = 'Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian'
den = 'Estimate!!SEX AND AGE!!Total population'

# Make a new column dividing Asian population column by Total population column
dp05_df['Asian percentage'] = dp05_df[num] / dp05_df[den]

# Sort on the new column
dp05_df.sort_values('Asian percentage', ascending=False)[
    ['Geographic Area Name', 'Asian percentage']
].head(10)

Now the story is very different. Hawaii leads by a wide margin, followed by California and New Jersey. The raw count and the percentage tell very different stories — a theme we will return to throughout this book.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.9 Putting It All Together
Here is the complete, clean loading pipeline for this dataset in a single cell. This is a pattern you will repeat at the start of almost every analysis: load with the right parameters, verify the shape, and inspect the first few rows.

dp05_df = pd.read_csv(
    'ACS2019_DP05_state.csv',
    header=1,
    na_values=['(X)', '*****', '-', '+', '***'],
    usecols=lambda col: 'Estimate' in col or 'Geographic Area Name' in col
)

print(dp05_df.shape)
dp05_df.head()

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.10 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Fix the header problem





Load ACS2019_DP05_state.csv without using the header parameter. Observe the problem. Then use both strategies (the read_csv parameter approach and the post-hoc approach) to fix it. Verify that both produce the same result.
Solution:

# Strategy 1: Make read_csv do it
dp05_v1 = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv', header=[1])

# Strategy 2: Fix it after
dp05_v2 = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv')
dp05_v2.columns = dp05_v2.loc[0]
dp05_v2 = dp05_v2.drop(index=0)

# Both should have the same shape
print(dp05_v1.shape, dp05_v2.shape)










NoteExercise 2: Handle multiple missing value codes





Load the data with header=[1] but without the na_values parameter. Use the .replace() method after loading to convert all six types of Census missing value codes into NaN. Verify that the numeric columns now have the correct float64 data type.
Solution:

dp05_df = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv', header=[1])

# Replace all missing value codes
missing_codes = ['(X)', '*****', '-', '+', '***', 'N']
dp05_df = dp05_df.replace({code: np.nan for code in missing_codes})

dp05_df.head()










NoteExercise 3: Filter to “Estimate” columns only





Using the usecols parameter inside read_csv, load only columns that contain the string “Estimate” plus the “Geographic Area Name” column. Then do the same thing using a post-hoc filter. Verify both approaches produce DataFrames with the same number of columns.
Solution:

# Strategy 1: usecols
dp05_v1 = pd.read_csv(
    'ACS2019_DP05_state.csv',
    header=1,
    na_values=['(X)', '*****', '-', '+', '***'],
    usecols=lambda col: 'Estimate' in col or 'Geographic Area Name' in col
)

# Strategy 2: post-hoc
dp05_v2 = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv', header=1,
                        na_values=['(X)', '*****', '-', '+', '***'])
keep = [c for c in dp05_v2.columns if 'Estimate' in c or 'Geographic Area Name' in c]
dp05_v2 = dp05_v2[keep]

print(dp05_v1.shape, dp05_v2.shape)










NoteExercise 4: Sort by Asian population





Sort the dp05_df DataFrame by the “Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian” column. What states have the largest Asian population?
Solution:

cols = ['Geographic Area Name', 'Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian']

dp05_df.sort_values(
    by='Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian',
    ascending=False
).loc[:, cols].head(10)










NoteExercise 5: Compute and sort on a new column





States with a large Asian population may simply have large populations. Compute the percentage of the population that is Asian by dividing the Asian population column by the total population column. Store as “Asian percentage.” Sort on this new column and show the top 5 and bottom 5 states.
Solution:

num = 'Estimate!!RACE!!Total population!!One race!!Asian'
den = 'Estimate!!SEX AND AGE!!Total population'

dp05_df['Asian percentage'] = dp05_df[num] / dp05_df[den]

# Top 5
print("Top 5:")
print(dp05_df.sort_values('Asian percentage', ascending=False)[
    ['Geographic Area Name', 'Asian percentage']
].head(5))

# Bottom 5
print("\nBottom 5:")
print(dp05_df.sort_values('Asian percentage', ascending=True)[
    ['Geographic Area Name', 'Asian percentage']
].head(5))










NoteExercise 6: Explore other read_csv parameters





Read the pandas documentation for read_csv. Experiment with these parameters:

dtype: Force specific columns to a type (e.g., keep FIPS codes as strings).
index_col: Set a column as the DataFrame’s index during loading.
nrows: Load only the first n rows — useful for previewing very large files.

When might each be useful?
Solution:

# nrows lets you peek at a huge file without loading it all
test_df = pd.read_csv('ACS2019_DP05_state.csv', header=1, nrows=5)
test_df.head()

dtype is essential when numeric-looking columns (like ZIP codes 06510 or FIPS codes 08031) should be treated as strings to preserve leading zeros. index_col saves a separate .set_index() call when you know which column should be the index. nrows is invaluable for previewing files with millions of rows without exhausting your computer’s memory.

title: 1  Loading Data
text:
1.11 Looking Ahead
You now know how to load a CSV file with control over headers, missing values, column selection, and data types. In Chapter 2, you will learn how to summarize the rows of a DataFrame using groupby-aggregation — one of the most powerful and commonly used operations in data analysis.

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.1 Setup
In Chapter 1 you learned to load and inspect a single dataset. But raw data rarely answers questions directly — you need to summarize it. How many cancer deaths occurred in each year? What was the average across states? What state had the most heart disease deaths?
This chapter introduces groupby-aggregation, an extremely common and very powerful mechanism for summarizing data. The idea is deceptively simple: split your data into groups that share some common value, apply a summary function to each group, and combine the results back into a single table. Mastering this pattern will serve you in virtually every data analysis you undertake.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

idx = pd.IndexSlice

pd.options.display.max_columns = 200

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.2 The Data
We will work with weekly mortality data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), which records deaths by cause for each U.S. state and week from 2014 through 2023. See Chapter 15 for details.

deaths_df = pd.read_csv(
    'CDC_deaths.csv',
    parse_dates=['Week date']
)

deaths_df.head()

Notice the parse_dates parameter: it tells read_csv to convert the “Week date” column from a string into a proper Timestamp object. We will explore temporal data in depth in Chapter 9.

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.3 Understanding Repeated Values
Before summarizing, you need to understand the structure of your data. The .value_counts() method counts how many times each unique value appears in a column.

deaths_df['State'].value_counts()

There are 52 “states” in this data: the 50 states plus the District of Columbia and Puerto Rico. Each state has observations for each week of each year.

deaths_df['Year'].value_counts()

Some years have more observations than others. Why? The .sort_index() method, chained after .value_counts(), sorts the results by the index (in this case, the year) rather than by count.

deaths_df['Week'].value_counts().sort_index()

There are 104 observations of a 53rd week. Since we have 52 states and this happens in two years, that is 52 × 2 = 104. Let’s verify: which years and dates have a 53rd week?
Here we use the .loc selector to do Boolean indexing — selecting rows that match a condition — while simultaneously selecting specific columns.

deaths_df.loc[
    deaths_df['Week'] == 53,  # Select the rows that are 53rd weeks
    ['Week date', 'Year']     # Select these columns
].value_counts()              # Count the values

The 53rd weeks end in early January 2015 and early January 2021, which technically belong to the 2014 and 2020 epidemiological years. This kind of calendrical irregularity is very common in real data.






TipExplore before you aggregate



Before running any groupby, spend time understanding how values repeat in your data. Unexpected duplicates or missing groups will silently produce wrong summaries.

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.4 Building Intuition: The Manual Way
What were the total cancer deaths in the United States in 2014? You could answer this manually by filtering to 2014 and selecting the “Malignant neoplasms” column.

# Make a "baby DataFrame" for the year 2014 and the column "Malignant neoplasms"
cancer_2014 = deaths_df.loc[
    deaths_df['Year'] == 2014,  # Rows
    ['Malignant neoplasms']      # Columns
]

print(len(cancer_2014))
cancer_2014

There are 2,756 rows of data. Let’s verify: 2014 is one of the years with 53 weeks and there are 52 states, so 52 × 53 = 2,756. Good — the numbers match our expectations.

53 * 52

Now apply a function. We want the total, so we .sum() the Series.

cancer_2014.sum()

To get totals for every year, you would need to repeat this process — a tedious and error-prone loop.

# Make an empty container to store results
total_deaths_d = {}

# Loop through the year values of 2014 to 2023
for year in range(2014, 2024):
    # Make the baby DataFrame for cancer deaths in the year
    cancer_deaths = deaths_df.loc[deaths_df['Year'] == year, 'Malignant neoplasms']

    # Sum the values and store the result
    total_deaths_d[year] = cancer_deaths.sum()

total_deaths_d

This works, but programmers are — first and foremost — lazy!

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.5 Groupby-Aggregation
The brilliance of groupby-aggregation is that you can accomplish the same thing in a single line, which both saves time and reduces the chances of errors.

deaths_df.groupby(['Year']).agg({'Malignant neoplasms': 'sum'})

You will almost always use groupby in combination with .agg(). Let’s break the operation into its three conceptual steps.

2.5.1 Splitting with .groupby()
The .groupby() operation splits the data into groups. The most important concept you need to internalize is that when you do a groupby, you are making a “baby DataFrame” where the rows all share something in common — whatever value you grouped on.

deaths_gb_year = deaths_df.groupby(['Year'])

What is this groupby object? Not a whole lot to look at on its own.

deaths_gb_year

But you can peek inside it with .get_group() to retrieve the baby DataFrame for a specific year.

deaths_gb_year.get_group(2023)



2.5.2 Applying a Function
Now that we have baby DataFrames for each year, we want to apply some function that reduces all the data down to a single summary value. In our cancer example, we are adding up 2,756 rows of state-week numbers into a single total.

deaths_gb_year.get_group(2014)['Malignant neoplasms'].sum()

We could do this for each year.

for year in range(2014, 2024):
    print(year, deaths_gb_year.get_group(year)['Malignant neoplasms'].sum())

You can apply just about any numeric aggregation function. Some common ones:

len — Number of rows in the baby DataFrame
sum — Total value of a column
mean — Average value of a column
min and max — Minimum and maximum values
count — Count of non-null values


deaths_gb_year['Malignant neoplasms'].apply(np.mean)



2.5.3 Combining with .agg()
The .agg() function serves to both apply and combine the results on a GroupBy object. There are several strategies for using .agg().
Single column, single function: Pass an aggregation dictionary where the key is the column name and the value is the function.

deaths_df.groupby(['Year']).agg({'Diabetes mellitus': 'sum'})

You can chain a .plot() call directly onto this result for a quick visualization. We will cover visualization in depth starting in Chapter 6.

deaths_gb_year['Diabetes mellitus'].agg('sum').plot(kind='bar')

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.6 Multiple Aggregation Functions
You can pass a list of aggregation functions to apply multiple summaries to the same column at once.

deaths_gb_year['Diabetes mellitus'].agg(['mean', 'min', 'max', 'count', 'sum'])

You can also pass a list of numpy functions, which is more explicit. Note how the function name becomes the column name.

deaths_gb_year['Diabetes mellitus'].agg([np.mean, np.min, np.max, len, np.sum])


2.6.1 Aggregation Dictionaries
The real power comes when you want to apply different functions to different columns. An aggregation dictionary maps column names to functions (or lists of functions).

agg_d = {
    'Diabetes mellitus': [np.mean, np.min, np.max, len, np.sum],
    'Diseases of heart': ['sum', 'mean']
}

deaths_gb_year.agg(agg_d)

This returns a DataFrame with a MultiIndex on the columns — the first level is the column name and the second level is the function name.

agg_d = {
    'Diabetes mellitus': [np.mean, min, max, len, np.sum],
    'Septicemia': [np.mean, np.median, min, max, np.sum]
}

deaths_agg_year = deaths_gb_year.agg(agg_d)
deaths_agg_year

You can access a specific value from the MultiIndexed columns by passing a tuple.

deaths_agg_year.loc[2014, ('Diabetes mellitus', 'sum')]


deaths_agg_year[('Septicemia', 'sum')]



2.6.2 Simplifying MultiIndex columns
You can drop the top level of the MultiIndex and relabel the columns for clarity.

deaths_agg_year.columns = deaths_agg_year.columns.droplevel(0)

deaths_agg_year.columns = [
    'mean_diabetes', 'min_diabetes', 'max_diabetes',
    'count_diabetes', 'total_diabetes',
    'mean_septicemia', 'median_septicemia', 'min_septicemia',
    'max_septicemia', 'total_septicemia'
]

deaths_agg_year.head()

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.7 Grouping by Multiple Values
You can group on more than one column by passing a list to .groupby().

disease_cols = deaths_df.columns[6:]
disease_cols


deaths_gb_year_state = deaths_df.groupby(['Year', 'State'])

Create an aggregation dictionary that sums each disease column.

disease_agg_d = {col: 'sum' for col in disease_cols}

deaths_gb_year_state_agg = deaths_gb_year_state.agg(disease_agg_d)
deaths_gb_year_state_agg

The index is now a MultiIndex comprised of tuples of (Year, State). You can access any row by passing the tuple to .loc.

deaths_gb_year_state_agg.loc[(2020, 'Colorado')].sort_values()

This shows deaths in Colorado in 2020, sorted by cause. COVID-19 was a leading cause, exceeding both cancer and heart disease in some calculations.

2.7.1 Accessing levels of a MultiIndex
You can access one level of a MultiIndex using slicing. All state-level deaths in 2014:

deaths_gb_year_state_agg.loc[2014]

To access the other level — all years for a single state — you need the IndexSlice helper.

idx = pd.IndexSlice

deaths_gb_year_state_agg.loc[idx[:, 'California'], 'Alzheimer disease']


deaths_gb_year_state_agg.loc[idx[:, 'California'], ['Cerebrovascular diseases']]

This is an extremely powerful pattern for slicing MultiIndexed data.

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.8 Pivot Tables
You may have encountered pivot tables in spreadsheet applications. Pandas has a similar function that reshapes data while simultaneously aggregating it.

allcause_pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    data=deaths_df,
    index="State",
    columns="Week",
    values="Influenza and pneumonia",
    aggfunc='sum'
)

allcause_pivot.T.plot(legend=False)

You can also make MultiIndexed pivot tables by passing lists of columns.

lower_respiratory_pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    data=deaths_df,
    index=["State", "Year"],
    columns="Week",
    values="Chronic lower respiratory diseases",
    aggfunc="sum"
)

lower_respiratory_pivot

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.9 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Annual “All Cause” deaths





Perform a groupby-aggregation to compute the total “All Cause” deaths by year. How much higher were deaths in 2020 than 2019?
Solution:

all_cause = deaths_df.groupby('Year').agg({'All Cause': 'sum'})
all_cause


diff = all_cause.loc[2020, 'All Cause'] - all_cause.loc[2019, 'All Cause']
pct = diff / all_cause.loc[2019, 'All Cause'] * 100
print(f"Difference: {diff:,.0f} deaths ({pct:.1f}% increase)")










NoteExercise 2: Weekly flu and pneumonia patterns





Make a pivot table with the Week as an index, Year as columns, and the total “Influenza and pneumonia” deaths as values. What is the deadliest week of the year on average for flu and pneumonia?
Solution:

flu_pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    data=deaths_df,
    index='Week',
    columns='Year',
    values='Influenza and pneumonia',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

# Average across years for each week
flu_pivot.mean(axis=1).sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)

The deadliest weeks tend to be in early January (weeks 1–4), corresponding to the peak of flu season.









NoteExercise 3: Top heart disease state





Perform a groupby-aggregation to compute the total number of “Diseases of heart” deaths by state across all years. What state had the most heart disease deaths from 2014 through 2023?
Solution:

heart_by_state = deaths_df.groupby('State').agg({'Diseases of heart': 'sum'})
heart_by_state.sort_values('Diseases of heart', ascending=False).head(5)

California is likely at the top, reflecting its large population.









NoteExercise 4: Heart disease in Colorado





What is the total number of heart disease deaths in Colorado from 2014 to the present?
Solution:

co_hearts = deaths_df.loc[
    deaths_df['State'] == 'Colorado'
].groupby('Year').agg({'Diseases of heart': 'sum'})

co_hearts










NoteExercise 5: Comparing diabetes deaths in 2019 and 2020





Perform a groupby-aggregation on Year and State and compute the total “Diabetes mellitus” deaths. Use slicing to compare diabetes deaths in 2019 and 2020 for Utah and Colorado.
Solution:

diabetes_agg = deaths_df.groupby(['Year', 'State']).agg({'Diabetes mellitus': 'sum'})

for state in ['Colorado', 'Utah']:
    d2019 = diabetes_agg.loc[(2019, state), 'Diabetes mellitus']
    d2020 = diabetes_agg.loc[(2020, state), 'Diabetes mellitus']
    print(f"{state}: 2019={d2019:,.0f}, 2020={d2020:,.0f}, change={d2020-d2019:+,.0f}")

title: 2  Aggregating and Summarizing
text:
2.10 Looking Ahead
Groupby-aggregation is a powerful tool for summarizing data, but it operates on a single dataset. In Chapter 3, you will learn how to combine multiple datasets together using concatenation and merging so that you can enrich your analyses with additional context — like joining demographic data to health data to compute per-capita rates.

title: 3  Combining Data
text:
3.1 Setup
So far you have worked with single datasets. In practice, the data you need is almost always spread across multiple files or sources. This chapter introduces two fundamental operations for combining data: concatenation (stacking datasets with the same columns on top of each other) and merging (joining datasets that share a common identifier column). These operations correspond to adding rows and adding columns, respectively.
We will use baseball data to illustrate both operations. Baseball has a long tradition of meticulous statistical record-keeping, and its datasets provide a clean and intuitive context for learning these skills. So we are going to take a break from last week’s very happy data about death statistics to work with baseball data this week.
The Retrosheet game logs and Lahman Database are the two most famous baseball datasets. Much of the data you see on sites like Baseball-Reference is derived from these sources.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import os

pd.options.display.max_columns = 200
pd.options.display.float_format = '{:,.2f}'.format

title: 3  Combining Data
text:
3.2 Setting and Resetting the Index
Before combining data, you need to understand how pandas uses the index. The index is the row label — by default an integer starting at 0, but it can be set to any column.
.reset_index() (docs) turns an index back into a regular column. .set_index() (docs) turns a column into the index.

batting_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/Batting.csv')

batting_df.tail()

Make a pivot table of annual runs by team (review from Chapter 2).

team_annual_hrs = pd.pivot_table(
    data=batting_df,
    index='yearID',
    columns='teamID',
    values='R',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

team_annual_hrs.shape


team_annual_hrs.loc[1998:].dropna(axis='columns')

Reset the index to turn “yearID” back into a column.

reset = team_annual_hrs.reset_index()
reset

Set it back.

reset.set_index('yearID', inplace=True)
reset


reset.loc[2021].dropna().sort_values(ascending=False)







TipWhen to set an index



Set a column as the index when you will frequently use it for lookups (like dates or unique identifiers). Reset the index when you need that column available for merging or groupby operations.

title: 3  Combining Data
text:
3.3 Concatenation
A concat lets you add rows on top of each other when the columns are the same or similar. Think of it as stacking two spreadsheets that have the same column headers.

3.3.1 Checking the data first
Let’s start by loading a single season of game log data.

gamelog_1921 = pd.read_csv('Retrosheet/GL1921.TXT')
gamelog_1921.head(2)

The data does not have column names — pandas assumes the first row contains headers. We need to tell read_csv there is no header row.

gamelog_1921 = pd.read_csv('Retrosheet/GL1921.TXT', header=None)
gamelog_1921.head(2)

Now the columns are numbered 0 to 160, but the Retrosheet documentation indexes columns starting from 1. To avoid future confusion, let’s fix this now.

gamelog_1921.columns = range(1, 162)
gamelog_1921.head(2)

What is the “shape” of this data? The .shape attribute returns the number of rows and columns as a tuple.

gamelog_1921.shape

The 1921 data has 1,229 rows (games) and 161 columns. Compare to 2021 — almost twice as many games because of league expansion, but still 161 columns.

gamelog_2021 = pd.read_csv('Retrosheet/GL2021.TXT', header=None)
gamelog_2021.columns = range(1, 162)
gamelog_2021.shape

The 2020 season was shortened by the COVID-19 pandemic.

gamelog_2020 = pd.read_csv('Retrosheet/GL2020.TXT', header=None)
gamelog_2020.shape

A quick concatenation of two seasons:

pd.concat([gamelog_1921, gamelog_2021]).shape



3.3.2 Looping through files
With 151 season files, loading them one by one is impractical. The os.listdir function takes a directory path and returns a list of filenames. We will use it on the “Retrosheet” folder.

sorted(os.listdir('Retrosheet/'))

First, verify that all files have the same number of columns — a critical check before concatenating.

for file in os.listdir('Retrosheet/'):
    if 'gl' in file.lower():
        _df = pd.read_csv('Retrosheet/' + file, header=None)
        if _df.shape[1] != 161:
            print(f"WARNING: {file} has {_df.shape[1]} columns")

If nothing prints, all 151 files have exactly 161 columns. Now load them all into a list.

gamelog_list = []

for file in sorted(os.listdir('Retrosheet/')):
    if file.startswith('GL'):
        _df = pd.read_csv('Retrosheet/' + file, header=None)
        _df.columns = range(1, 162)
        gamelog_list.append(_df)

Develop a practice of constantly verifying that your operations match your expectations. We had 151 files, so our list should have 151 objects.

len(gamelog_list)







WarningValidate before concatenating



Always verify that all DataFrames you plan to concatenate have the same number of columns. A single mismatched file will introduce NaN values across hundreds of thousands of rows.




3.3.3 Using pd.concat
Concatenate all seasons into a single DataFrame.

gamelogs_df = pd.concat(objs=gamelog_list)

gamelogs_df.shape



3.3.4 Managing indices after concatenation
One interesting (and frustrating) consequence of concatenation is that the indices from each parent DataFrame are preserved. This means index values like 0, 1, 2, … repeat for every season.

gamelogs_df.loc[13]

There are 151 rows named 13 — one from each season. This is usually a problem. You have two options.
Option 1: Use .reset_index(drop=True) after concatenating. The drop=True parameter discards the old index rather than turning it into a column.

gamelogs_df_reset = gamelogs_df.reset_index(drop=True)

gamelogs_df_reset.loc[2000]

Option 2: Use ignore_index=True inside pd.concat. This is the lazy (and preferred) option because it handles the problem at the point of concatenation.

gamelogs_df_ignore = pd.concat(
    objs=gamelog_list,
    ignore_index=True
)

gamelogs_df_ignore.loc[1000]



3.3.5 Filtering concatenated data
With all 151 seasons in a single DataFrame, you can do interesting cross-historical analyses. Let’s extract the year from the date column and find all Boston Red Sox vs. New York Yankees games.

gamelogs_df_reset['year'] = gamelogs_df_reset[1].astype(str).str.slice(0, 4)


# Boston vs. Yankees games
away_condition = gamelogs_df_reset[4].isin(['BOS', 'NYA'])
home_condition = gamelogs_df_reset[7].isin(['BOS', 'NYA'])

sox_yankees_df = gamelogs_df_reset[home_condition & away_condition]
sox_yankees_df


pd.pivot_table(
    data=sox_yankees_df,
    index=4,
    columns=7,
    values=[10, 11],
    aggfunc='mean'
)

title: 3  Combining Data
text:
3.4 Merging
A merge lets you add columns next to each other when the rows share a common identifier. This is more commonly known as a join. Pandas has both a merge function and a .join() method, but merge is more general and is what we will use.

3.4.1 Preparing for a merge
Load the People and Batting tables from the Lahman database.

people_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/People.csv')
print(people_df.shape)
people_df.head(2)


batting_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/Batting.csv').sort_values(['playerID', 'yearID'])
print(batting_df.shape)
batting_df.head(10)



3.4.2 Identifying join keys
What columns do these two DataFrames have in common? Set operations on the column names reveal the shared keys.

set(batting_df.columns) & set(people_df.columns)

The playerID column is present in both. But do they cover the same set of players?

print(f"Unique players in batting: {len(set(batting_df['playerID']))}")
print(f"Unique players in people: {len(set(people_df['playerID']))}")
print(f"Players in both: {len(set(batting_df['playerID']) & set(people_df['playerID']))}")

There are more people in the People table than in the Batting table because the People table includes pitchers, managers, and other non-batters.


3.4.3 Checking for duplicates
Are there repeated player IDs in either table? Understanding this is critical for predicting the shape of your merged result.

people_df['playerID'].value_counts()

Each player appears exactly once in People — it is a lookup table. But in Batting:

batting_df['playerID'].value_counts()

Players appear multiple times because the Batting table has one row per player-season (and even per player-team within a season for traded players). This means our merge will be a one-to-many join: each person row will match multiple batting rows.


3.4.4 Types of joins
Pandas supports four types of joins through the how parameter.

3.4.4.1 Left join
A left join preserves all rows in the left DataFrame. Rows from the right that do not match get NaN.

people_batting_left = pd.merge(
    left=people_df,
    right=batting_df,
    left_on='playerID',
    right_on='playerID',
    how='left'
)

print(people_df.shape, batting_df.shape, people_batting_left.shape)
people_batting_left.head(12)



3.4.4.2 Right join
A right join preserves all rows in the right DataFrame. Rows from the left that do not match get NaN.

people_batting_right = pd.merge(
    left=people_df,
    right=batting_df,
    left_on='playerID',
    right_on='playerID',
    how='right'
)

print(people_df.shape, batting_df.shape, people_batting_right.shape)
people_batting_right.head(9)



3.4.4.3 Outer join
An outer join preserves all rows from both DataFrames. Non-matching rows get NaN on the side that did not match.

people_batting_outer = pd.merge(
    left=people_df,
    right=batting_df,
    left_on='playerID',
    right_on='playerID',
    how='outer'
)

print(people_df.shape, batting_df.shape, people_batting_outer.shape)



3.4.4.4 Inner join
An inner join preserves only rows where the key exists in both DataFrames. Everything else is dropped.

people_batting_inner = pd.merge(
    left=people_df,
    right=batting_df,
    left_on='playerID',
    right_on='playerID',
    how='inner'
)

print(people_df.shape, batting_df.shape, people_batting_inner.shape)




3.4.5 Validating the merge
Always check the shape of your merged DataFrame. Ask yourself: how many rows and columns should the result have? If the number of rows is unexpectedly large, you may have a many-to-many merge — which is almost never what you intended.

title: 3  Combining Data
text:
3.5 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Do the big concatenation





Concatenate all 151 seasons of gamelog data in gamelog_list together. Make sure the index is unique. What is the .shape of the combined DataFrame?
Solution:

gamelogs_all = pd.concat(gamelog_list, ignore_index=True)
print(gamelogs_all.shape)










NoteExercise 2: Find extreme matches





The Retrosheet documentation explains that column 10 is the visiting team score and column 11 is the home team score. Column 18 is the game attendance.
What team scored the most runs ever in a single game? What was the highest-attended game?
Solution:

# Highest visiting team score
max_visitor_idx = gamelogs_df_reset[10].idxmax()
print("Highest visiting score:")
print(gamelogs_df_reset.loc[max_visitor_idx, [1, 4, 7, 10, 11]])










NoteExercise 3: Make a pivot table (review)





Column 3 is the day of the week. Column 13 is whether the game was a night or day game. Column 18 is attendance. Make a pivot table showing average attendance by day of the week and day/night status.
Solution:

pd.pivot_table(
    data=gamelogs_df_reset,
    index=3,
    columns=13,
    values=18,
    aggfunc='mean'
).loc[['Sun', 'Mon', 'Tue', 'Wed', 'Thu', 'Fri', 'Sat']]










NoteExercise 4: Which country produced the most Hall of Famers?





Join the “HallOfFame” and “People” tables from the Lahman database. Filter to inducted players. Do an aggregation to count Hall of Famers by birth country.
Solution:

halloffame_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/HallOfFame.csv')

# Filter to inducted players
inducted = halloffame_df[
    (halloffame_df['inducted'] == 'Y') &
    (halloffame_df['category'] == 'Player')
]

# Join
joined = pd.merge(
    left=inducted,
    right=people_df,
    on='playerID',
    how='left'
)

# Aggregate
joined.groupby('birthCountry').agg({'playerID': 'nunique'}).sort_values(
    'playerID', ascending=False
).head(10)










NoteExercise 5: Three-way join





Which schools produce the highest-paid players? Load the “Salaries”, “Schools”, and “CollegePlaying” tables from the Lahman database. Develop a strategy for joining all three and answering the question.
Solution:

salaries_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/Salaries.csv')
college_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/CollegePlaying.csv')
schools_df = pd.read_csv('Lahman/Schools.csv')

# Step 1: Join salaries to college playing on playerID
sal_college = pd.merge(salaries_df, college_df, on='playerID', how='inner')

# Step 2: Join schools on schoolID
sal_college_school = pd.merge(sal_college, schools_df, on='schoolID', how='left')

# Step 3: Aggregate
sal_college_school.groupby('name_full').agg(
    {'salary': 'mean'}
).sort_values('salary', ascending=False).head(10)

This is definitely biased towards schools that produce a small number of players with enormous contracts.

title: 3  Combining Data
text:
3.6 Looking Ahead
You can now combine datasets by stacking rows (concatenation) or joining on shared keys (merging). In Chapter 4, you will learn to reshape a single dataset between wide and long formats using stack, unstack, melt, and pivot — operations that are essential for both analysis and visualization.

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.1 Setup
The previous chapters taught you to load, summarize, and combine data. This chapter addresses a subtler but equally important skill: reshaping data between wide and long formats. The same underlying data can be arranged in multiple ways, and the shape you need depends on what you want to do with it. Groupby-aggregation and pivot tables often produce wide tables that are easy to read, but visualization libraries like seaborn expect long tables with one observation per row. Being able to move fluently between these formats is essential.
We will use monthly passenger traffic data from Denver International Airport (DIA) to illustrate these operations. The COVID-19 pandemic caused dramatic disruptions to air travel, making this dataset both analytically interesting and motivating.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

idx = pd.IndexSlice

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.2 The Data

4.2.1 Formulate your question
How did the COVID-19 shutdowns impact passenger and flight traffic at Denver International Airport?


4.2.2 Read in data

passenger_df = pd.read_csv('passengers.csv')
passenger_df.head()


passenger_df.tail()



4.2.3 Check the packaging
The data should span from 1995 (when DIA opened) through late 2021. A simple but fragile way to check this is to sort the “Month” column — but string sorting on dates gives wrong results (e.g., “April” comes before “January” alphabetically). Instead, convert to proper Timestamp objects using pd.to_datetime.

passenger_df['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(passenger_df['Month'])

passenger_df['Month'].min(), passenger_df['Month'].max()



4.2.4 Clean up the data
Make the “Month” column the index and drop the “Cargo” column, which is not relevant to our passenger analysis.

passenger_df.set_index('Month', inplace=True)


passenger_df.drop(columns=['Cargo'], inplace=True)


passenger_df.head()



4.2.5 Validate against an external source
These statistics are collected by the DIA authority. The U.S. Department of Transportation’s Bureau of Transportation Statistics also publishes historical passenger statistics by airport. Let’s compare.

transstats_df = pd.read_excel(
    'transtats_passengers.xlsx',
    sheet_name=0,
    header=1
)

# Filter out annual total rows and null rows
transstats_df = transstats_df[transstats_df['Month'] != "TOTAL"]
transstats_df.dropna(subset=['Month'], inplace=True)

transstats_df.head()

Combine the Year and Month columns into a proper datetime. This is a common data-cleaning task when dates are split across columns.

string_dates = transstats_df[['Year', 'Month']].apply(
    lambda x: '{Year}-{Month:02d}'.format(**x), axis=1
)

transstats_df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(string_dates)
transstats_df.set_index('Date', inplace=True)

transstats_df.head()

Join them together for comparison (practice from Chapter 3).

dia_bts_merge_df = pd.merge(
    left=passenger_df,
    right=transstats_df,
    left_index=True,
    right_on='Date',
    how='right'
)

dia_bts_merge_df.tail()

Calculate the correlation between the monthly totals from each dataset. Not perfect, but more than high enough to trust.

dia_bts_merge_df[['Grand', 'TOTAL']].corr()



4.2.6 Make a plot
Visualize the monthly total passenger data. We will go into visualization in much more detail in Chapter 6 and beyond.

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

ax = passenger_df['Grand'].plot()

ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2001-09-01'), color='r', linestyle='--', linewidth=1, zorder=-1)
ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-01'), color='r', linestyle='--', linewidth=1, zorder=-1)



4.2.7 Try an easy solution
How much did passenger traffic decrease in March 2020 compared to March 2019?

total_march_2019 = passenger_df.loc['2019-03-01', 'Grand']
total_march_2020 = passenger_df.loc['2020-03-01', 'Grand']

"{0:.1%}".format((total_march_2020 - total_march_2019) / total_march_2019)

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.3 Stacking: Wide to Long
If you have a DataFrame and want to convert its columns into index levels, use pandas’s .stack() (docs, user guide).
Our passenger_df is currently in wide format: each airline is a column, each row is a month, and the values are passenger counts.

passenger_df.head()

Stacking converts this into a long format — a MultiIndexed Series with Month at the first level and airline at the second level.

passenger_stack_df = passenger_df.stack()

passenger_stack_df.head(10)

Turn this MultiIndexed Series into a flat DataFrame with .reset_index(), then rename the columns for clarity.

passenger_stack_df = passenger_stack_df.reset_index()

passenger_stack_df.head(2)


passenger_stack_df.rename(
    columns={
        'level_1': 'Airline',
        0: 'Passengers'
    },
    inplace=True
)

passenger_stack_df.head()

This long format — where each row is a single observation (one airline, one month, one passenger count) — is what Hadley Wickham calls tidy data. Each variable forms a column, each observation forms a row, and each type of observational unit forms a table.






TipTidy data and visualization



Seaborn and many other visualization libraries expect tidy (long) data. If a plot function asks for x, y, and hue parameters that all reference column names, you need your data in long format.

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.4 Unstacking: Long to Wide
Now we want to reverse the process — take our long DataFrame and return it to wide format. Set the columns you want as index levels using .set_index(), then call .unstack() to pivot one level into columns.

passenger_unstack_df = passenger_stack_df.set_index(['Month', 'Airline'])

passenger_unstack_df


passenger_unstack_df = passenger_unstack_df.unstack('Airline')

passenger_unstack_df.head()

This output is a little annoying because it created a MultiIndex on the columns — see the “Passengers” above the airline names? Accessing United requires passing passenger_unstack_df[('Passengers', 'United')]. You can remove this extra level with .droplevel().

passenger_unstack_df.droplevel(0, axis='columns')

The cleaner alternative is to unstack a Series rather than a DataFrame. Access the single value column as a Series first, then unstack.

passenger_stack_s = passenger_stack_df.set_index(['Month', 'Airline']).loc[:, 'Passengers']

passenger_stack_s


passenger_stack_s.unstack(1)

This produces a clean, simple wide DataFrame without the MultiIndex on columns.

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
section: 4.5 Melting: Another Path from Wide to Long
text:
4.5 Melting: Another Path from Wide to Long
The pd.melt function offers a more explicit interface for going from wide to long. You specify which columns remain as identifiers (id_vars) and which get “melted” into value rows.
First, reset the index to get “Month” as a column.

passenger_df_reset = passenger_df.reset_index()
print(passenger_df_reset.shape)
passenger_df_reset.head()

Note the shape: 321 rows × 9 columns. After melting, 8 of those 9 columns (the airlines) should become rows. So we should get approximately 321 × 8 = 2,568 rows.

321 * 8


passengers_melt_df = pd.melt(
    frame=passenger_df_reset,
    id_vars=['Month'],
    var_name='Airline',
    value_name='Count'
)

print(passengers_melt_df.shape)
passengers_melt_df.head()

You can also pass explicit value_vars to melt only specific columns.

passengers_melt_df = pd.melt(
    frame=passenger_df_reset,
    id_vars=['Month'],
    value_vars=['American', 'Delta', 'Frontier', 'Other',
                'Southwest', 'United', 'United Express', 'Grand'],
    var_name='Airline',
    value_name='Count'
)

print(passengers_melt_df.shape)
passengers_melt_df.head()







WarningChoose your tool



.stack() and .melt() both go from wide to long, but .melt() gives you more control over which columns to include and how to name the result. Use .melt() when you want to reshape a subset of columns; use .stack() when you want to convert all value columns.

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.6 Unmelting with Pivot
A melted DataFrame can be “unmelted” with the .pivot() method. Pass the column names you want for the index, columns, and values.

passengers_melt_df.pivot(index='Month', columns='Airline', values='Count').head()

You can also pivot the other way — airlines as rows, months as columns.

passengers_melt_df.pivot(index='Airline', columns='Month', values='Count')

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.7 Summary of Reshaping Operations
Here is a quick reference for how the reshaping operations relate to each other:

Wide → Long: .stack() or pd.melt()
Long → Wide: .unstack() or .pivot() or pd.pivot_table()
Wide → Long → Wide should return you to where you started (a good sanity check)

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.8 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Filter the melted data (review)





Filter the passengers_melt_df data down to only “United.” Identify the months with the most and fewest passengers.
Solution:

united = passengers_melt_df[passengers_melt_df['Airline'] == 'United']

print("Most passengers:")
print(united.sort_values('Count', ascending=False).head(3))

print("\nFewest passengers:")
print(united.sort_values('Count', ascending=True).head(3))










NoteExercise 2: Total passengers by airline (review)





Do a groupby-aggregation or pivot table on passengers_melt_df to compute total passengers by airline since 1994. Which airline has carried the most passengers through DIA?
Solution:

passengers_melt_df[passengers_melt_df['Airline'] != 'Grand'].groupby('Airline').agg(
    {'Count': 'sum'}
).sort_values('Count', ascending=False)










NoteExercise 3: Convert back to wide data





Turn the tidy data in passengers_melt_df back into wide format using either a .pivot() or an .unstack() approach. Try making the months into columns too.
Solution:

# Using pivot
wide = passengers_melt_df.pivot(index='Month', columns='Airline', values='Count')
wide.head()










NoteExercise 4: Tidy the CDC data





Load the CDC deaths data from Chapter 2. Identify which columns should be id_vars (identifiers like State, Year, Week) and which should be value_vars (the disease columns). Use pd.melt or .stack() to reshape it into tidy format. How many rows does the result have? Does that match your prediction?
Solution:

deaths_df = pd.read_csv('CDC_deaths.csv', parse_dates=['Week date'])

id_cols = ['State', 'Year', 'Week', 'Week date']
value_cols = [c for c in deaths_df.columns if c not in id_cols]

deaths_tidy = pd.melt(
    deaths_df,
    id_vars=id_cols,
    value_vars=value_cols,
    var_name='Cause',
    value_name='Deaths'
)

print(f"Original shape: {deaths_df.shape}")
print(f"Tidy shape: {deaths_tidy.shape}")
print(f"Expected rows: {deaths_df.shape[0]} × {len(value_cols)} = {deaths_df.shape[0] * len(value_cols)}")










NoteExercise 5: Untidy and re-tidy





Take the tidy CDC data from Exercise 4 and pivot it back into wide format. Verify that the result has the same shape as the original DataFrame.
Solution:

deaths_wide = deaths_tidy.pivot_table(
    index=id_cols,
    columns='Cause',
    values='Deaths',
    aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()

print(f"Re-widened shape: {deaths_wide.shape}")

title: 4  Reshaping and Tidying
text:
4.9 Looking Ahead
You now have a complete toolkit for shaping data: loading, aggregating, combining, and reshaping. In Chapter 5, we step back from code to think about what makes a data visualization clear, honest, and effective — principles that will guide every chart you build in the rest of this book.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.1 Setup
The first four chapters of this book focused on computation: loading, aggregating, combining, and reshaping data. This chapter shifts focus to communication. A DataFrame can be perfectly structured and a calculation perfectly correct, but if the resulting visualization or narrative is confusing, misleading, or cluttered, the analysis fails to achieve its purpose.
This chapter covers the principles of effective data visualization, introduces matplotlib’s core plotting interface, and addresses the fundamentals of writing clearly about data. You will learn to create basic plots, label them properly, annotate them with context, and begin developing the critical eye that distinguishes a helpful chart from a harmful one.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.2 The Lifecycle of a Plot
Making a chart is not a single step but a cycle. You start with a question, choose a chart type, produce a draft, evaluate what is confusing or misleading, revise, and repeat. The first draft is almost never the final product, and that is fine. Treat visualization as an iterative design process, just as you would treat writing or coding.
The matplotlib Lifecycle of a Plot tutorial illustrates this principle well. A good plot is not born — it is built, step by step.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.3 Visual Processing and Perceptual Rankings
Not all visual encodings are equally effective. Research by Cleveland and McGill, summarized in Schwabish’s Better Data Visualizations, shows that humans decode some visual channels more accurately than others. Ranked from most to least accurate for quantitative comparisons:

Position along a common scale (points on a shared axis)
Position along non-aligned scales
Length
Angle and slope
Area
Volume, color saturation, and color hue

This hierarchy explains why bar charts and dot plots (which encode data as position) tend to outperform pie charts (which encode data as angle and area) for precise comparisons. When you choose a chart type, you are choosing a perceptual channel — choose wisely.






TipThe data-ink ratio



Edward Tufte’s principle of maximizing the “data-ink ratio” — the proportion of ink devoted to data rather than decoration — is a useful heuristic. Gridlines, 3D effects, gradient fills, and elaborate legends often reduce clarity rather than improve it. Ask yourself: does this visual element help the reader understand the data, or does it just look pretty?

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.4 Making Your First Plot
Let’s build a visualization using the DIA passenger data from Chapter 4.

passenger_df = pd.read_csv('passengers.csv')
passenger_df['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(passenger_df['Month'])
passenger_df.set_index('Month', inplace=True)
passenger_df.drop(columns=['Cargo'], inplace=True)

A minimal line plot requires only one call.

passenger_df['Grand'].plot(figsize=(12, 6))

This default plot communicates the general trend, but it has several problems. There are no axis labels, no title, no indication of what the y-axis units are, and no context for the dramatic drop that is visible around 2020. Let’s improve it systematically.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.5 Matplotlib’s Object-Oriented Interface
We will use matplotlib’s object-oriented coding style, which gives you explicit control over every element of your visualization. The key idea: you create a Figure (the canvas) and one or more Axes (the individual plots) using plt.subplots(), then call methods on those objects to customize them.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

passenger_df['Grand'].plot(ax=ax)

ax.set_xlabel('Date')
ax.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers')
ax.set_title('Denver International Airport Total Passenger Traffic, 1995–2021')

The f object is the entire figure. The ax object is the subplot (called an “Axes” in matplotlib’s terminology). You will use ax.set_xlabel(), ax.set_ylabel(), ax.set_title(), and many other methods to customize the plot.






WarningUnlabeled axes are unacceptable



In this book and in professional practice, every chart must have labeled axes with units. A y-axis that says “Count” is better than nothing, but “Monthly Passengers” is better still. Include units (dollars, degrees Fahrenheit, percentage points) whenever applicable.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.6 Adding Context with Annotations
Data does not exist in a vacuum. Major events — policy changes, natural disasters, pandemics — shape the patterns in your data. Annotations help the reader connect the visual pattern to the real-world cause.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 6))

passenger_df['Grand'].plot(ax=ax, color='steelblue', linewidth=1.5)

# Vertical lines for major events
ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2001-09-01'), color='r', linestyle='--', linewidth=1, zorder=-1)
ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-01'), color='r', linestyle='--', linewidth=1, zorder=-1)

# Text annotations
ax.annotate('September 11', xy=(pd.Timestamp('2001-09-01'), 3_500_000),
            xytext=(pd.Timestamp('2003-06-01'), 4_500_000),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='red'),
            fontsize=10, color='red')

ax.annotate('COVID-19', xy=(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-01'), 1_000_000),
            xytext=(pd.Timestamp('2017-01-01'), 1_500_000),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='purple'),
            fontsize=10, color='purple')

ax.set_xlabel('Date')
ax.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers')
ax.set_title('Denver International Airport Total Passenger Traffic, 1995–2021')

Now the chart tells a story: steady growth with a dip after 9/11, followed by strong recovery, then a historic collapse during COVID-19.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.7 Multiple Subplots
You can create figures with multiple subplots by adjusting the nrows and ncols parameters of plt.subplots().

f, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(
    nrows=1,
    ncols=2,
    figsize=(14, 5),
    sharey=True
)

passenger_df['United'].plot(ax=ax0, color='tab:blue')
ax0.set_title('United Airlines')
ax0.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers')

passenger_df['Southwest'].plot(ax=ax1, color='tab:orange')
ax1.set_title('Southwest Airlines')

f.suptitle('DIA Monthly Passengers by Airline', fontsize=14)
f.tight_layout()

The sharey=True parameter ensures both subplots use the same y-axis scale, enabling fair comparison. The f.tight_layout() call adjusts spacing to prevent labels from overlapping.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.8 Common Graphical Mistakes
As you develop your visualization skills, watch for these common pitfalls.
Truncated axes. Starting a bar chart’s y-axis at a value other than zero exaggerates differences. A bar that appears twice as tall should represent twice the value. Line charts are more forgiving because they emphasize change rather than magnitude, but be aware of the implicit argument you make when you zoom in on a narrow range.
Dual axes. Two y-axes on the same chart can imply a relationship between variables that does not exist. The apparent correlation between two lines can be manufactured by adjusting the scale of either axis. Use dual axes sparingly and with explicit justification.
Excessive decoration. Gridlines, 3D effects, gradient fills, and elaborate legends often reduce clarity. Every non-data element competes for the reader’s attention.
Missing context. A chart showing a dramatic upward trend is meaningless without knowing the baseline, the units, and the time period. Always include enough context for a reader to evaluate whether the pattern is meaningful.
Chart type mismatch. Using a pie chart to compare twelve categories, a bar chart to show a time series, or a scatter plot when you have only three data points — each of these forces the reader to work harder than necessary. Match the chart type to the structure of the data and the comparison you want to emphasize.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.9 Writing About Data
Visualization is only half of data communication. The other half is writing. Good data writing, as Nolan and Stoudt argue in Communicating with Data, has several characteristics.
Lead with the finding. Do not make the reader sift through methodology to find the conclusion. State the key finding in the first sentence, then provide the evidence. “DIA passenger traffic fell 93% between February and April 2020” is a finding. “We analyzed monthly passenger data from 1995 to 2021” is a methodology statement — useful, but secondary.
Use specific numbers. “Traffic decreased significantly” is vague. “Traffic fell from 5.3 million to 380,000 passengers” is concrete and verifiable. Specificity builds trust.
Acknowledge uncertainty. If a result is based on a sample, a model, or incomplete data, say so. Your credibility depends on being honest about what you know and what you do not know. We will explore uncertainty communication further in Chapter 11.
Write for your audience. A blog post for the general public requires different vocabulary and level of detail than a technical report for data scientists. We will explore audience analysis in Chapter 8.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.10 Crafting Sentences
Some specific writing advice for data communication, drawn from Schwabish, Nolan, and Stoudt.
Active voice over passive. Write “Colorado legalized cannabis in 2014” rather than “Cannabis was legalized in Colorado in 2014.” Active voice is shorter, clearer, and assigns agency.
Avoid hedging where the data is clear. If the result is statistically significant, say “spending differed between parties” rather than “spending may have differed between parties.” Reserve hedging for genuinely uncertain claims.
Define jargon on first use. If you must use a technical term, define it immediately. “The p-value (the probability of seeing this result if there were no real difference) was less than 0.001.”
Keep sentences short. If a sentence contains more than one data point, consider splitting it. “DIA served 5.3 million passengers in February 2020. By April, that number had fallen to 380,000.”

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.11 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Critique a visualization





Find a data visualization in a news article, social media post, or textbook. Identify three specific things the visualization does well and three things it could improve. Consider labels, scales, color choices, clutter, and whether the chart type matches the data.
Solution:
There is no single correct answer. The goal is to practice articulating why a design choice helps or hinders communication. Strong critiques reference the perceptual ranking hierarchy: “The pie chart uses angle to encode values, which is harder to read accurately than position. A bar chart would make the comparison clearer.”









NoteExercise 2: Improve a default plot





Load any dataset from the book’s repository. Create a default plot using .plot(). Then create a second version with at least five improvements: axis labels, a title, annotations, color choices, and a figsize adjustment. Compare the two.
Solution:

deaths_df = pd.read_csv('CDC_deaths.csv', parse_dates=['Week date'])

# Default
deaths_df.groupby('Year').agg({'Diseases of heart': 'sum'}).plot(kind='bar')


# Improved
f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
totals = deaths_df.groupby('Year').agg({'Diseases of heart': 'sum'})
totals.plot(kind='bar', ax=ax, color='indianred', legend=False)
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Total Deaths')
ax.set_title('Annual U.S. Heart Disease Deaths, 2014–2023')
ax.set_xticklabels(totals.index, rotation=45)
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

Removing the top and right spines reduces visual clutter and follows Tufte’s data-ink ratio principle.









NoteExercise 3: Write about a chart





Take the improved chart from Exercise 2 and write a three-sentence summary that leads with the finding, provides one supporting number, and notes a caveat.
Solution:
Example: “Heart disease deaths in the U.S. increased 5.4% from 2019 to 2020, the largest year-over-year jump in the past decade. Total deaths rose from approximately 657,000 to 692,000, a change that coincides with the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. This increase likely reflects both direct cardiovascular effects of COVID-19 and disruptions to routine cardiac care, though the CDC data does not distinguish between these mechanisms.”









NoteExercise 4: Perceptual ranking





You want to compare the total number of passengers carried by each airline at DIA. Rank the following chart types from most to least effective for this comparison: pie chart, horizontal bar chart, bubble chart, table of numbers. Explain your reasoning using the Cleveland and McGill hierarchy.
Solution:

Horizontal bar chart — encodes values as position/length along a common scale (top of the hierarchy).
Table of numbers — exact values with no perceptual encoding required; excellent for precision but poor for quick pattern recognition.
Bubble chart — encodes values as area, which humans judge less accurately than length.
Pie chart — encodes values as angle and area, the least accurate channels in the hierarchy. Particularly poor when there are many slices of similar size.

title: 5  Clarity in Data Communication
text:
5.12 Looking Ahead
With a foundation in both data shaping and visualization principles, you are ready to start building specific chart types. In Chapter 6, you will take a deep dive into matplotlib’s object-oriented interface by visualizing distributions with histograms — your first systematic encounter with the shape of data values themselves.

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.1 Setup
In Chapter 5 you learned the principles of effective visualization. Now you will apply those principles to a specific chart type: the histogram. Histograms show the distribution of a single continuous variable — how frequently different values occur. Understanding the shape of your data is one of the most important steps in any analysis, and histograms are the workhorse tool for doing so.
This chapter also serves as your deep engagement with matplotlib’s object-oriented interface. Rather than calling convenience functions, you will create Figure and Axes objects explicitly and manipulate them. Every visual element in a matplotlib chart is an object with methods you can call, and understanding this architecture gives you fine-grained control over every pixel of your visualization.
import numpy as np
np.set_printoptions(suppress=True)
import pandas as pd

idx = pd.IndexSlice

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100
Load the Boulder and Broomfield weather data. Note the pipe (|) delimiter — not all “CSV” files use commas.
boulder_df = pd.read_csv('boulder_weather.csv', sep='|')
boulder_df.head()
broomfield_df = pd.read_csv('broomfield_weather.csv', sep='|')
broomfield_df.head()

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.2 Setting Up Matplotlib
When we use matplotlib within a Jupyter Notebook, we need to run a special command called a “cell magic” so that the images it generates are included directly in the notebook. Without %matplotlib inline, you would need to call plt.show() or plt.savefig() to see your visualizations.
We construct and manage plots through matplotlib’s pyplot (docs) interface.

%matplotlib inline

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

title: 6  Histograms
section: 6.3 Creating and Customizing Figures and Subplots
text:
6.3 Creating and Customizing Figures and Subplots
This is our first stop on the object-oriented coding style. Before we plot any data, let’s understand what a figure is by creating an empty one.

f, ax = plt.subplots()

The f object is the Figure — the entire canvas. The ax object is the Axes — a single subplot within the figure. Calling f returns the (now-empty) visualization.

f

The Figure contains an enormous number of methods and attributes. The .get_axes() method retrieves the subplot objects within the figure. There is only one subplot here, so it is a list of one item.

f.get_axes()

The ax object has its own methods and attributes. The .get_children() method shows all the components of this “empty” plot: spines for the four borders (top, bottom, right, left), the XAxis and YAxis with tick labels 0.0 to 1.0, empty Text objects for the xlabel, ylabel, and title, and a Rectangle patch object.

ax.get_children()


6.3.1 Mutating objects
Every visual element is an object you can modify. The Rectangle has a height, width, and origin.

rect = ax.get_children()[-1]
rect.get_height(), rect.get_width(), rect.get_xy()

We can mutate this Rectangle — color it red and shrink it.

rect.set_facecolor('red')
rect.set_height(.5)
rect.set_width(.5)

Check the figure again. The rectangle is now red and half-sized.

f







NoteExercise 1: Mutate a patch





Mutate the rect object (docs) so that it has an edgecolor of green and a linewidth of 10. Display the result.
Solution:

rect.set_edgecolor('green')
rect.set_linewidth(10)

f




This exercise may seem trivial, but it demonstrates the core principle: matplotlib visualizations are built from objects, and you customize them by calling methods on those objects. Every bar in a histogram, every line in a time series, and every point in a scatter plot is an object you can inspect and modify.

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.4 One-Dimensional Histogram
Now let’s make a real histogram. First, retrieve the temperature values from the Boulder weather data as a numpy array using the .values attribute.

boulder_temps = boulder_df['TEMP'].values
boulder_temps

Create an empty figure and subplot.

f, ax = plt.subplots()

Plot a histogram of boulder_temps in the ax object. The .hist method returns three values: the count of values in each bin, the boundaries of each bin, and the BarContainer object that populates the axes.

ax.hist(boulder_temps)

But where is the plot? It was drawn into ax, which lives inside f. Check f.

f

If you create the figure, subplot, and histogram in the same cell (which we will do from now on), the figure appears automatically. You can silence the extra output from .hist() by adding a semicolon at the end of the line.

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(boulder_temps);  # Semicolon silences the bin counts and boundaries


6.4.1 Choosing the number of bins
The bins parameter controls how many bars the histogram displays. Too few bins obscure the shape of the distribution; too many create noise. Experiment with different values.
Few bins (5): The shape is barely visible.

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(boulder_temps, bins=5);

Moderate bins (25): We start to see a bimodal distribution — two peaks corresponding to winter and summer temperatures. This is exactly the kind of insight histograms are designed to reveal.

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(boulder_temps, bins=25);

Many bins (100): The bimodal pattern is clearer, but this is probably more granular than a typical histogram needs to be.


6.4.2 Labeling axes
One of the most essential visualization practices is to label your axes. A histogram without labels forces the reader to guess what they are looking at.

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(boulder_temps, bins=25, facecolor='#CFB87C')

ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Days)')
ax.set_title('Temperatures in Boulder, Colorado (2010–2021)')

The #CFB87C color is CU Boulder’s gold. Choosing meaningful colors — team colors, brand colors, or colorblind-friendly palettes — is a small detail that elevates your visualizations.






NoteExercise 2: Broomfield histogram





Create an empty figure and subplot, extract the “TEMP” values from broomfield_df, make a histogram with 25 bins, and label your axes.
Solution:

broomfield_temps = broomfield_df['TEMP'].values

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(broomfield_temps, bins=25, facecolor='#CFB87C')

ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Days)')
ax.set_title('Temperatures in Broomfield, Colorado (2010–2021)')

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.5 Skewed Data
The weather data has nice statistical properties — it is approximately normally distributed (or bimodal, reflecting seasons). Many real-world datasets do not share this property. Income, population, case counts, and web traffic often show a strong right skew: many small values and a few extremely large ones.
Load the Colorado county-level COVID data.

co_covid_df = pd.read_csv('co_county_covid.csv')
co_covid_df.head()


co_covid_df['Cases'].describe()

Make a histogram. There is an enormous number of county-months with zero or few cases and a handful with thousands. The first bar dominates the chart, making it uninformative.

covid_cases = co_covid_df['Cases'].values

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(covid_cases, bins=50)

ax.set_xlabel('Cases')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Monthly)')
ax.set_title('COVID-19 Cases in Colorado Counties')

Even adding more bins does not improve this visualization. The fundamental problem is that a linear scale cannot accommodate data spanning several orders of magnitude.

6.5.1 Log transformations
A common approach to handling skewed data is to use logarithmic bins and axes. Rather than bin boundaries being linearly spaced (0, 1000, 2000, …), they are geometrically spaced (1, 10, 100, 1000, …).
Numpy provides two functions for generating evenly-spaced sequences. linspace creates linearly-spaced values and geomspace creates geometrically-spaced values.

np.linspace(0, 10000, 11)


np.geomspace(1, 10000, 11)  # Start at 10^0 = 1, end at 10^4 = 10000

Pass geometric bin boundaries to the bins parameter, then set logarithmic scales on the axes.

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(covid_cases, bins=np.geomspace(1, 10000, 11))

ax.set_xlabel('Cases')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Monthly)')
ax.set_title('Monthly COVID-19 Cases in Colorado Counties')

ax.set_xscale('log')

Adding a log scale to the y-axis as well creates a log-log plot, which reveals the underlying distributional shape.

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(covid_cases, bins=np.geomspace(1, 10000, 11))

ax.set_xlabel('Cases')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Monthly)')
ax.set_title('Monthly COVID-19 Cases in Colorado Counties')

ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_yscale('log')







WarningLog of zero is undefined



You cannot take the logarithm of zero, and np.geomspace cannot start at zero. Filter your data to positive values before applying a log transform, or use np.geomspace(1, ...) to start at 1.
