6.5.2 Why log-binned data matters
Different probability distributions look similar on linear scales but have very different shapes on log scales. This is important because the distributional shape determines which statistical tools are appropriate. Here is a demonstration with four simulated distributions.

from sklearn.preprocessing import minmax_scale
_size = 1000

random_exponential = minmax_scale(np.random.exponential(size=_size), feature_range=(0, 1))
random_lognormal = minmax_scale(np.random.lognormal(size=_size), feature_range=(0, 1))
random_pareto = minmax_scale(np.random.pareto(a=1, size=_size), feature_range=(0, 1))
random_power = minmax_scale(np.random.power(a=5, size=_size), feature_range=(0, 1))

On linear axes, these distributions look quite similar.

f, (ax0, ax1, ax2, ax3) = plt.subplots(nrows=1, ncols=4, figsize=(16, 4))

ax0.hist(random_exponential, bins=25)
ax1.hist(random_lognormal, bins=25)
ax2.hist(random_pareto, bins=25)
ax3.hist(random_power, bins=25)

ax0.set_title('Exponential')
ax1.set_title('Log-normal')
ax2.set_title('Pareto')
ax3.set_title('Power')

f.suptitle('Linear axes and bins', fontweight='bold')
f.tight_layout()

On log axes with geometric bins, they look very different — each has a distinctive signature shape.

f, (ax0, ax1, ax2, ax3) = plt.subplots(
    nrows=1, ncols=4, figsize=(16, 4),
    subplot_kw={'xscale': 'log'}
)

ax0.hist(random_exponential, bins=np.geomspace(1e-3, 1e0, 25))
ax1.hist(random_lognormal, bins=np.geomspace(1e-3, 1e0, 25))
ax2.hist(random_pareto, bins=np.geomspace(1e-4, 1e0, 25))
ax3.hist(random_power, bins=np.geomspace(1e-3, 1e0, 25))

ax0.set_title('Exponential')
ax1.set_title('Log-normal')
ax2.set_title('Pareto')
ax3.set_title('Power')

f.suptitle('Log axes and geometric bins', fontweight='bold')
f.tight_layout()







NoteExercise 3: Log-log histogram of deaths





Extract the “Deaths” values from co_covid_df. Make a histogram with geometrically-spaced bins, log-scaled axes, and labeled axes.
Solution:

covid_deaths = co_covid_df['Deaths'].values

f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(covid_deaths, bins=np.geomspace(1, 50, 25))

ax.set_title('COVID Deaths, Colorado, 2020–2022')
ax.set_xlabel('Deaths')
ax.set_ylabel('Count')

ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_yscale('log')

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.6 Visualizing Multiple Histograms
We often want to compare distributions across groups. Let’s compare temperatures in Boulder and Broomfield. Because the datasets cover slightly different date ranges and have different sizes, we first inner-join them on the date column so we are comparing the same days.

boulder_df.shape, broomfield_df.shape


both_df = pd.merge(
    left=boulder_df,
    right=broomfield_df,
    left_on='DATE',
    right_on='DATE',
    how='inner',
    suffixes=('_BOULDER', '_BROOMFIELD')
)

both_df.head()


boulder_temps = both_df['TEMP_BOULDER'].values
broomfield_temps = both_df['TEMP_BROOMFIELD'].values


6.6.1 Option 1: Side-by-side subplots
Use ncols=2 to create two subplots side by side. The sharey=True parameter ensures they use the same y-axis scale for fair comparison.

f, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(
    nrows=1, ncols=2,
    figsize=(8, 4),
    sharey=True
)

ax0.hist(boulder_temps, bins=25)
ax0.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax0.set_ylabel('Count (Days)')
ax0.set_title('Boulder, Colorado')

ax1.hist(broomfield_temps, bins=25)
ax1.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax1.set_title('Broomfield, Colorado')

f.suptitle('Temperatures 2010–2021', fontweight='heavy')
f.tight_layout()



6.6.2 Option 2: Grouped bars on a single plot
Stack the two numpy arrays together and pass them to .hist() with color and label parameters.

stacked_arrays = np.stack([boulder_temps, broomfield_temps], axis=1)
boulder_temps.shape, broomfield_temps.shape, stacked_arrays.shape


f, ax = plt.subplots()

ax.hist(
    stacked_arrays,
    bins=25,
    histtype='bar',
    color=['tab:red', 'tab:blue'],
    label=['Boulder', 'Broomfield']
)

ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Days)')
ax.set_title('Temperatures (2010–2021)', fontweight='heavy')
ax.legend(prop={'size': 10})



6.6.3 Option 3: Stacked bars
Setting stacked=True layers one distribution on top of the other. This is perceptually harder to compare but shows the combined total.

f, ax = plt.subplots()

ax.hist(
    stacked_arrays,
    bins=15,
    histtype='bar',
    stacked=True,
    color=['tab:red', 'tab:blue'],
    label=['Boulder', 'Broomfield']
)

ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Days)')
ax.set_title('Temperatures (2010–2021)', fontweight='heavy')
ax.legend(prop={'size': 10})



6.6.4 Option 4: Cumulative histogram
A cumulative histogram shows the running total. This is useful for answering questions like “on what fraction of days was the temperature below 50°F?”

f, ax = plt.subplots()

ax.hist(
    stacked_arrays,
    bins=15,
    histtype='bar',
    cumulative=True,
    color=['tab:red', 'tab:blue'],
    label=['Boulder', 'Broomfield']
)

ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Days, cumulative)')
ax.set_title('Temperatures (2010–2021)', fontweight='heavy')
ax.legend(prop={'size': 10})



6.6.5 Small multiples with pandas
You can use pandas to generate a grid of histograms grouped by a categorical variable — one histogram per county, for example.

f, axs = plt.subplots(nrows=13, ncols=5, figsize=(10, 20))

co_covid_df.plot.hist(
    column='Cases',
    by=['County'],
    ax=axs,
    legend=False
);

f.tight_layout()

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.7 Pandas Histogram Methods
Everything above involved extracting numpy arrays from pandas. You can also create histograms directly from pandas objects.
The .hist() method on a Series produces a quick histogram.

both_df['TEMP_BOULDER'].hist(grid=False, bins=25)

Calling .hist() on a DataFrame makes one histogram per column.

both_df[['TEMP_BOULDER', 'TEMP_BROOMFIELD']].hist()

The .plot.hist() method offers additional options like stacking and horizontal orientation.

both_df[['TEMP_BOULDER', 'TEMP_BROOMFIELD']].plot.hist(stacked=True)


both_df[['TEMP_BOULDER', 'TEMP_BROOMFIELD']].plot.hist(orientation='horizontal')

You can also combine pandas plotting with your own figure setup by passing ax as a parameter.

f, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(ncols=2, figsize=(8, 4))

both_df[['TEMP_BOULDER', 'TEMP_BROOMFIELD']].plot.hist(stacked=True, ax=ax0, legend=False)
both_df[['TEMP_BOULDER', 'TEMP_BROOMFIELD']].plot.hist(orientation='horizontal', ax=ax1)

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.8 Two-Dimensional Histograms
You can visualize the joint distribution of two variables with a 2D histogram. This works like a terrain map: darker colors represent lower counts and brighter colors represent higher counts.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))

counts, xedges, yedges, im = ax.hist2d(
    x=boulder_temps,
    y=broomfield_temps,
    bins=25
)

ax.set_xlabel('Temperatures in Boulder (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Temperatures in Broomfield (F)')
f.colorbar(im, ax=ax)

Unsurprisingly, two nearby cities have very similar temperatures. All the values lie along the diagonal — there are no days when it is 80°F in Boulder and 20°F in Broomfield.

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.9 Exercises






NoteExercise 4: COVID cases and deaths histograms





Make a figure with two side-by-side subplots. The left subplot should show a histogram of COVID cases and the right subplot should show COVID deaths. Use appropriate bins and consider whether log scaling is needed.
Solution:

f, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

ax0.hist(co_covid_df['Cases'].values, bins=np.geomspace(1, 10000, 20))
ax0.set_xscale('log')
ax0.set_xlabel('Cases')
ax0.set_ylabel('Count')
ax0.set_title('COVID Cases')

ax1.hist(co_covid_df['Deaths'].values, bins=np.geomspace(1, 100, 20))
ax1.set_xscale('log')
ax1.set_xlabel('Deaths')
ax1.set_ylabel('Count')
ax1.set_title('COVID Deaths')

f.suptitle('Colorado County-Month COVID-19 Distributions')
f.tight_layout()










NoteExercise 5: Descriptive statistics and histograms





Compute the mean, median, and standard deviation of Boulder’s temperature data. Add vertical lines to your histogram at the mean and median. Do they fall in the same place? What does the difference (or similarity) tell you about the symmetry of the distribution?
Solution:

mean_temp = np.mean(boulder_temps)
median_temp = np.median(boulder_temps)

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.hist(boulder_temps, bins=25, facecolor='#CFB87C', edgecolor='white')
ax.axvline(mean_temp, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean: {mean_temp:.1f}°F')
ax.axvline(median_temp, color='blue', linestyle='--', label=f'Median: {median_temp:.1f}°F')
ax.legend()
ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (F)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (Days)')
ax.set_title('Boulder Temperatures with Mean and Median')

If the mean and median are close, the distribution is approximately symmetric. If they diverge, the distribution is skewed. For bimodal data like temperatures, both measures fall between the two peaks and may be close to each other even though neither peak is near the center.









NoteExercise 6: Four-variable subplot grid





Create a figure with four subplots (2 rows, 2 columns) showing histograms of four different weather variables from the Boulder data (e.g., TEMP, PRCP, WDSP, MAX). Label each subplot appropriately.
Solution:

f, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))

vars_and_labels = [
    ('TEMP', 'Temperature (°F)'),
    ('PRCP', 'Precipitation (in)'),
    ('WDSP', 'Wind Speed (mph)'),
    ('MAX', 'Max Temperature (°F)')
]

for ax, (col, label) in zip(axs.flatten(), vars_and_labels):
    vals = boulder_df[col].dropna().values
    ax.hist(vals, bins=25, color='steelblue', edgecolor='white')
    ax.set_xlabel(label)
    ax.set_ylabel('Count')

f.suptitle('Boulder Weather Variable Distributions', fontsize=14)
f.tight_layout()

title: 6  Histograms
text:
6.10 Looking Ahead
Histograms show the distribution of one continuous variable. In Chapter 7, you will learn to compare distributions across categories — Democrats vs. Republicans, different world regions, incumbents vs. newcomers — using seaborn’s powerful categorical plotting functions.

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.1 Setup
In Chapter 6 you visualized the distribution of a single continuous variable. In practice, you often want to compare distributions across two or more categories: How does spending differ between political parties? Do different office buildings have different budgets? Do arms imports vary across world regions? These questions involve a continuous variable (spending, imports) and a categorical variable (party, building, region), and they call for a family of visualizations known as categorical plots — or catplots.
This chapter introduces the seaborn library’s catplot function, which provides a unified interface for bar plots, box plots, violin plots, strip plots, and more. You will also learn to perform basic statistical tests to evaluate whether observed differences between categories are meaningful or likely due to chance. The central idea tying these plots together is that you have two or more categories of data and want to compare their distributions — in other words, analyzing the relationships between continuous and categorical variables of interest.
import numpy as np
np.set_printoptions(suppress=True)

import pandas as pd
idx = pd.IndexSlice
pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import seaborn as sb

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.2 Refresher: Statistical Significance Testing
Before diving into the visualizations, let’s review the intuition behind a statistical significance test. The independent samples t-test evaluates whether two groups have different means.

from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

Create two random distributions with different means to see what a “real” difference looks like.

rand_first = np.random.normal(loc=3, scale=2, size=100)
rand_second = np.random.normal(loc=6, scale=2, size=100)

# Visualize both
f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(rand_first, bins=15, alpha=.5)
ax.hist(rand_second, bins=15, alpha=.5)

# Add vertical lines for their means
ax.axvline(rand_first.mean(), c='tab:blue', lw=2)
ax.axvline(rand_second.mean(), c='tab:orange', lw=2)


ttest_ind(rand_first, rand_second)

A small p-value (well below 0.05) tells us that the observed difference is unlikely to have occurred by chance. A large p-value would tell us we cannot reject the null hypothesis that the two groups have the same mean.

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.3 The Data: House Expenditures
We will use U.S. House of Representatives office expenditure data from ProPublica for the third quarter of 2021. Each row is a detailed disbursement — a single spending transaction — by a congressional office.

house_21q3_df = pd.read_csv('2021Q3-house-disburse-detail.csv')
house_21q3_df.head()


7.3.1 Understanding the data structure
The “SORT SEQUENCE” column includes subtotals and other aggregated rows. We want only the detail-level transactions to avoid double-counting.

house_21q3_df['SORT SEQUENCE'].value_counts()

In addition to representatives’ offices, there are offices for various officers and administrators. The BIOGUIDE_ID is a standardized identifier for every senator and representative going back to 1790. Rows without a BIOGUIDE_ID are non-member offices.

house_21q3_df['BIOGUIDE_ID'].value_counts()



7.3.2 Filtering
Filter to only detail-level transactions for actual representatives.

c0 = house_21q3_df['SORT SEQUENCE'] == 'DETAIL'
c1 = house_21q3_df['BIOGUIDE_ID'].notnull()

print(f"Raw: {len(house_21q3_df):,} rows")
print(f"Filtered: {len(house_21q3_df[c0 & c1]):,} rows")

filtered_df = house_21q3_df[c0 & c1]

What is the average total spending per member’s office?

filtered_df.groupby('BIOGUIDE_ID').agg({'AMOUNT': 'sum'}).mean()



7.3.3 Joining member data
To compare spending by party, we need to merge the expenditure data with member information that includes party affiliation, state, and other context.

members_df = pd.read_csv('house_members.csv')
members_df.head(2)


joined_df = pd.merge(
    left=filtered_df,
    right=members_df,
    left_on='BIOGUIDE_ID',
    right_on='bioguideID',
    how='left'
)

print(f"Members: {len(members_df)}, Filtered: {len(filtered_df):,}, "
      f"Joined: {len(joined_df):,}")
joined_df.head(2)



7.3.4 Top-spending state delegations
Which state delegations spend the most? A simple aggregation shows raw totals, but larger delegations naturally spend more.

joined_df.groupby('state-fullname').agg({'AMOUNT': 'sum'}).sort_values(
    'AMOUNT', ascending=False
)

Normalizing by the number of representatives in each delegation reveals spending per member.

top_spenders = joined_df.groupby('state-fullname').agg(
    {'AMOUNT': 'sum', 'bioguideID': 'nunique'}
)
top_spenders['per_rep'] = top_spenders['AMOUNT'] / top_spenders['bioguideID']
top_spenders.sort_values('per_rep', ascending=False).head(10)

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.4 Bar Plots
Seaborn’s catplot function with kind='bar' shows the mean (or other summary statistic) for each category, with error bars representing the 95% confidence interval by default.

sb.catplot(
    data=joined_df,
    x='caucus',
    y='AMOUNT',
    kind='bar',
    order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=['tab:blue', 'tab:red']
)







WarningTransaction-level vs. office-level



This chart shows the average transaction amount, not the average total spending per office. A party whose members make many small transactions will look different from one making fewer large ones, even if total spending is identical. Always be clear about what unit of analysis your chart represents.


To get office-level totals, aggregate first and then merge.

total_spending = joined_df.groupby('BIOGUIDE_ID').agg({'AMOUNT': 'sum'})
total_spending.head()


spending_joined = pd.merge(
    left=total_spending.reset_index(),
    right=members_df,
    left_on='BIOGUIDE_ID',
    right_on='bioguideID',
    how='left'
)

spending_joined.head()

Now the bar plot represents total quarterly spending per legislator.

sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    x='caucus',
    y='AMOUNT',
    kind='bar',
    order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=['tab:blue', 'tab:red']
)

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.5 Box Plots
Box plots show the full distribution rather than just the mean. The box spans the interquartile range (25th to 75th percentile), the line inside is the median, and the whiskers extend to 1.5 times the interquartile range. Points beyond the whiskers are outliers.

g = sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    y='AMOUNT',
    x='caucus',
    kind='box',
    order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=['tab:blue', 'tab:red']
)

Box plots reveal that spending distributions are right-skewed (the median is below the center of the box) and that there are several high-spending outliers in both parties.

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.6 Violin Plots
Violin plots combine a box plot with a kernel density estimate, showing the full shape of the distribution. They are especially useful when distributions are multimodal (have multiple peaks).

sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    x='caucus',
    y='AMOUNT',
    kind='violin',
    order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=sb.color_palette(['blue', 'red'])
)

title: 7  Categorical Plots
section: 7.7 Adding a Second Category with hue
text:
7.7 Adding a Second Category with hue
The hue parameter subdivides each category by a second variable. Here we compare spending by party and by office building.

sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    x='office-building',
    y='AMOUNT',
    hue='caucus',
    kind='bar',
    estimator=np.mean,
    hue_order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=sb.color_palette(['tab:blue', 'tab:red']),
    height=3
)

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.8 Statistical Significance Testing
Visualizations show patterns, but they do not tell you whether the patterns are statistically significant. The independent samples t-test (ttest_ind from scipy.stats) tests whether two groups have the same mean.

7.8.1 Transaction-level test

dem_transactions = joined_df.loc[joined_df['caucus'] == 'D', 'AMOUNT']
rep_transactions = joined_df.loc[joined_df['caucus'] == 'R', 'AMOUNT']

print(f"Democratic transactions: {len(dem_transactions):,}")
print(f"Republican transactions: {len(rep_transactions):,}")


ttest_ind(dem_transactions, rep_transactions)



7.8.2 Legislator-level test

dem_office = spending_joined.loc[spending_joined['caucus'] == 'D', 'AMOUNT']
rep_office = spending_joined.loc[spending_joined['caucus'] == 'R', 'AMOUNT']

ttest_ind(dem_office, rep_office)







TipReport effect sizes, not just p-values



The p-value tells you whether a difference exists, but not how large it is. Always report the actual difference (e.g., “Democratic offices spent $42,000 more on average”) alongside the p-value. A statistically significant difference of $500 across hundreds of legislators may not be substantively meaningful.

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.9 Exploring Other Categories
The spending data contains many categorical variables beyond party.

sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    x='prior-congress',
    y='AMOUNT',
    hue='caucus',
    kind='bar',
    hue_order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=sb.color_palette(['tab:blue', 'tab:red']),
    height=4
)

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.10 Country Military Data
Let’s apply catplots to a different dataset: the World Bank’s World Development Indicators, which has data on military expenditures, armed forces size, and arms imports and exports for 266 countries. The .. values represent missing data.

military_df = pd.read_csv('country_military.csv', na_values='..')

military_df.columns = ['Country Name', 'Country Code', 'Series Name',
                        'Series Code'] + list(range(2010, 2021))

military_df.head()


7.10.1 Adding regional categories
To make catplots of military data, we need a categorical variable. The United Nations and World Bank publish regional classifications of countries.

regions_df = pd.read_csv('regional_classifications.csv')
regions_df.head()

After reshaping and joining (exercises left below), we can make catplots like this one comparing arms imports by UN region.

# This code assumes you have prepared a cat_military_df by pivoting
# the military data and joining with regions_df
# f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
# sb.boxplot(data=cat_military_df, x='Arms imports', y='UN Regions', ax=ax)
# ax.set_xscale('log')

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.11 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Strip and swarm plots





Use seaborn’s catplot to create a strip plot (kind='strip') and a swarm plot (kind='swarm') of total spending by party. How do these plots compare to bar and box plots? When might each be preferable?
Solution:

sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    x='caucus',
    y='AMOUNT',
    kind='strip',
    order=['D', 'R'],
    palette=['tab:blue', 'tab:red'],
    alpha=0.4
)

Strip plots show every data point, making them excellent for small datasets where you want to see the full distribution. Swarm plots arrange points to avoid overlap, which is clearer for moderately sized datasets but becomes slow for large ones. Bar plots are better for audiences expecting summary statistics; box plots are better when you want to highlight spread and outliers.









NoteExercise 2: Incumbency and spending





Create a Boolean column “incumbent” that is True when the “prior-congress” column is not 0. Then make a catplot comparing spending between incumbents and non-incumbents, subdivided by party using hue. Are incumbents bigger spenders?
Solution:

spending_joined['incumbent'] = spending_joined['prior-congress'] != 0

sb.catplot(
    data=spending_joined,
    x='incumbent',
    y='AMOUNT',
    hue='caucus',
    kind='bar',
    palette=['tab:blue', 'tab:red'],
    hue_order=['D', 'R']
)










NoteExercise 3: Top spending categories





Which spending categories (CATEGORY column in the disbursement data) account for the most total spending? Create a horizontal bar plot of the top 10 categories.
Solution:

cat_spending = joined_df.groupby('CATEGORY').agg({'AMOUNT': 'sum'})
top_cats = cat_spending.sort_values('AMOUNT', ascending=True).tail(10)

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
top_cats.plot(kind='barh', ax=ax, legend=False, color='steelblue')
ax.set_xlabel('Total Spending ($)')
ax.set_title('Top 10 House Spending Categories, Q3 2021')










NoteExercise 4: Who was the top spender in Travel?





Filter the joined data to the “TRAVEL” category. Which member’s office spent the most on travel? Research why that might be (hint: consider the member’s district location).
Solution:

travel = joined_df[joined_df['CATEGORY'] == 'TRAVEL']
travel_by_member = travel.groupby(['BIOGUIDE_ID', 'state-fullname']).agg({'AMOUNT': 'sum'})
travel_by_member.sort_values('AMOUNT', ascending=False).head(5)

Members representing distant districts (Alaska, Hawaii, Guam) tend to have the highest travel costs because flights to Washington, D.C. are longer and more expensive.









NoteExercise 5: Clean and melt the military data





Clean the military_df so the columns and series names are more interpretable. Drop the “Series Code” column. Pivot and melt the data so that the “Series Name” values become columns and “Country Name” and “Year” are ID variables. Then make a histogram of GDP values using log-scaled bins.
Solution:

# Drop Series Code
mil_clean = military_df.drop(columns=['Series Code'])

# Melt years into rows
mil_long = pd.melt(
    mil_clean,
    id_vars=['Country Name', 'Country Code', 'Series Name'],
    var_name='Year',
    value_name='Value'
)

# Pivot so each Series Name becomes a column
mil_pivot = mil_long.pivot_table(
    index=['Country Name', 'Country Code', 'Year'],
    columns='Series Name',
    values='Value'
).reset_index()

mil_pivot.head()










NoteExercise 6: Statistical significance across regions





Using the military data joined with regional classifications, test whether GDP for countries in the “Europe & Central Asia” region is significantly different from GDP in the “East Asia & Pacific” region using ttest_ind.
Solution:

# Assuming cat_military_df has been prepared with a 'GDP' column and 'UN Regions'
# europe = cat_military_df.loc[cat_military_df['UN Regions'] == 'Europe & Central Asia', 'GDP'].dropna()
# asia = cat_military_df.loc[cat_military_df['UN Regions'] == 'East Asia & Pacific', 'GDP'].dropna()
# ttest_ind(europe, asia)

Interpret the result by considering both the p-value and the actual difference in means. Even if the difference is statistically significant, consider whether the comparison is meaningful given the enormous within-region variation.

title: 7  Categorical Plots
text:
7.12 Looking Ahead
You have now compared distributions across categories using bar, box, violin, and strip plots. In Chapter 8, we step back from the code to think carefully about who your audience is and how that shapes every decision you make in data communication.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.1 Setup
The previous chapters in this part taught you to visualize distributions with histograms and compare them across categories with catplots. But a chart does not speak for itself. Every visualization is created for someone, and the choices you make — what to include, what to exclude, how to frame the finding, how much detail to provide — should be driven by who that someone is.
This chapter explores audience analysis: the practice of understanding your reader before you write. Knowing your audience changes every aspect of your data communication, from the chart type you choose to the vocabulary you use to the narrative structure you follow. We will use the DIA passenger and House expenditure data from earlier chapters to illustrate how the same underlying analysis produces very different outputs depending on the intended audience.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sb

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.2 Who Is Your Audience?
Andrew Abela, in Advanced Presentations by Design, argues that every data presentation should begin with three questions about the audience.

8.2.1 What does the audience already know?
A data scientist reviewing your work understands what a p-value means and expects to see one. A city council member may not know what a p-value is and would be better served by a plain-language statement like “This difference is very unlikely to be due to chance.” A general reader of a blog post falls somewhere in between and might benefit from a brief definition.
The level of existing knowledge determines your vocabulary, the amount of methodological detail you include, and how much context you need to provide for each chart.


8.2.2 What does the audience need to decide?
Data communication is rarely just informational — it almost always supports a decision. A DIA executive might need to decide whether to open a new terminal. A journalist might need to decide whether the passenger recovery trend is newsworthy. A congressional staffer might need to decide whether to recommend changes to an office’s spending practices.
The decision shapes what data matters. The executive cares about long-term capacity projections. The journalist cares about the narrative arc of decline and recovery. The staffer cares about line-item comparisons to peer offices. The same underlying dataset serves all three, but the analysis and presentation differ substantially.


8.2.3 What is the audience’s prior belief?
If your audience already believes that COVID-19 devastated air travel, a chart confirming that belief is unremarkable. The more interesting and useful contribution might be how quickly traffic recovered, or which airlines recovered fastest, or how DIA compared to other airports.
Understanding your audience’s priors helps you focus on what is genuinely new and useful rather than restating the obvious. Conversely, if your finding contradicts a widely held belief, you need to present stronger evidence and anticipate skepticism.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.3 Getting to the Core
Sejal Vora, in The Power of Data Storytelling, argues that you should be able to state your core finding in a single sentence before you create any visualization. This sentence becomes the organizing principle for everything else.
Consider the House expenditure data from Chapter 7. Several possible core findings emerge from the same data, and each would lead to a different presentation.

“Democratic and Republican offices spend similar amounts overall, but differ meaningfully in how they allocate across categories.”
“Members representing distant districts spend significantly more on travel than those near Washington, D.C.”
“Incumbent members spend 12% more than freshmen, regardless of party.”

Each of these core findings would produce a different chart, a different headline, and a different narrative structure. Before opening matplotlib, decide which story you are telling.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.4 Tailoring a Visualization
Let’s demonstrate with the DIA passenger data.

passenger_df = pd.read_csv('passengers.csv')
passenger_df['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(passenger_df['Month'])
passenger_df.set_index('Month', inplace=True)
passenger_df.drop(columns=['Cargo'], inplace=True)


8.4.1 For a technical audience
A data science team reviewing your analysis wants to see the full data, clear methodology, and reproducible code. You might produce a detailed time series with multiple airlines, consistent axis scales, and statistical annotations.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 6))

for airline in ['United', 'Southwest', 'Frontier']:
    passenger_df[airline].plot(ax=ax, label=airline, alpha=0.8)

ax.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers')
ax.set_xlabel('Date')
ax.set_title('DIA Monthly Passengers by Major Carrier, 1995–2021')
ax.legend()
ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-01'), color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

This chart is detailed and precise. It includes multiple series, uses consistent colors, and annotates the COVID-19 onset without editorial commentary. A technical audience would know to examine the relative recovery rates and the crossover between United and Southwest around 2010.


8.4.2 For a general audience
A blog post reader wants a clear takeaway, minimal jargon, and a chart that communicates the point at a glance. Simplify to a single line (total passengers) with prominent annotations and a descriptive title.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

passenger_df['Grand'].plot(ax=ax, color='steelblue', linewidth=2)

ax.annotate(
    'COVID-19\nshutdowns',
    xy=(pd.Timestamp('2020-04-01'), 500_000),
    fontsize=12,
    fontweight='bold',
    color='crimson'
)

ax.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('How COVID-19 Crushed Denver Airport Traffic', fontsize=14)
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.set_xlabel('')

The title is a finding, not a description. The y-axis is labeled with a plain-English phrase. The top and right spines are removed to reduce clutter. There is one annotation pointing to the key moment. A general reader can absorb the message in three seconds.


8.4.3 For a decision-maker
An airport executive needs to decide whether passenger traffic has recovered enough to justify expansion. The most useful chart is a comparison of current levels against a pre-pandemic baseline.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

# Compute a pre-pandemic baseline: average monthly traffic 2015-2019
baseline = passenger_df.loc['2015':'2019'].groupby(passenger_df.loc['2015':'2019'].index.month).mean()

# Plot 2021 actual vs. baseline
actual_2021 = passenger_df.loc['2021', 'Grand']
ax.plot(range(1, 13), baseline['Grand'].values, label='2015–2019 Average',
        color='gray', linestyle='--', linewidth=2)
ax.plot(actual_2021.index.month, actual_2021.values, label='2021 Actual',
        color='steelblue', linewidth=2, marker='o')

ax.set_xticks(range(1, 13))
ax.set_xticklabels(['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun',
                     'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec'])
ax.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers')
ax.set_title('2021 Recovery vs. Pre-Pandemic Baseline')
ax.legend()

The same data, the same underlying truth, but three very different presentations optimized for three very different readers.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.5 Narrative Structure
Data stories, like all stories, have a structure. Research by Segel and Heer, and later discussed by Bach, Stefaner, and others in Data-Driven Storytelling, identified common narrative patterns.

8.5.1 The Martini Glass
This pattern begins with a narrow, author-driven narrative (“here is the finding”) and then opens up to let the reader explore the data on their own. A blog post that leads with a chart and headline, then provides an interactive dashboard, follows this pattern. It works well for general audiences who benefit from being told the conclusion before they examine the evidence.


8.5.2 The Interactive Slideshow
This pattern alternates between author-driven slides and reader-driven exploration at each step. A scrollytelling article where the reader advances through annotated charts is the classic example. It works well for audiences who want to understand the methodology step by step.


8.5.3 The Drill-Down
This pattern starts with an overview and progressively reveals more detail. You might begin with total DIA traffic, then break down by airline, then by route, then by day of week. It works well for analytical audiences who want to explore the data at multiple levels.
Your choice of structure depends on the audience’s level of engagement and expertise. A general audience benefits from the Martini Glass (give them the conclusion first). A technical audience may prefer a drill-down (let them evaluate the methodology).

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.6 Communicating Data to an Audience
Drawing from Drucker, Huron, Kosara, Schwabish, and Diakopoulos in Data-Driven Storytelling, several principles guide effective data communication regardless of audience.
Annotation is argument. When you add an annotation to a chart — a label, an arrow, a highlighted region — you are making an argument about what the reader should pay attention to. Use annotations deliberately to guide the reader’s eye to the most important patterns.
Redundancy builds comprehension. Encoding the same information in multiple channels (color and label, position and size) makes the chart accessible to readers with different cognitive styles and to those with color vision deficiencies.
Simplicity is not dumbing down. Removing unnecessary gridlines, decimals, or chart elements does not make your analysis less rigorous. It makes it more communicable. Einstein reportedly said “Everything should be made as simple as possible, but not simpler.” The same applies to charts.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.7 Writing the First Draft
When you sit down to write about your data analysis, drawing from Nolan and Stoudt’s Communicating with Data:
Start with the finding. Your opening sentence should state the most important result. “DIA passenger traffic fell 93% between February and April 2020” is a finding. “We analyzed monthly passenger data from 1995 to 2021” is a methodology statement — useful, but secondary.
Use concrete numbers. Vague claims (“traffic dropped significantly”) are less compelling and less trustworthy than specific ones (“traffic dropped from 5.3 million to 380,000 passengers”).
Explain, don’t just show. A chart alone is not an argument. Tell the reader what the chart shows, why it matters, and what it means for the question at hand. Resist the temptation to let the chart “speak for itself.”
Anticipate objections. A sophisticated reader will wonder about seasonal effects, population growth, or airline mergers. Address these proactively — even briefly — to build credibility. If you don’t raise the obvious objections, the reader will assume you did not consider them.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.8 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Two versions of one chart





Choose a finding from the House expenditure data in Chapter 7. Create two versions of a chart communicating that finding: one for a congressional staffer who works with this data daily, and one for a constituent reading a local newspaper. Write a one-paragraph caption for each.
Solution:
For the staffer, include party breakdown, confidence intervals, dollar amounts, and a note about the data source and quarter. The caption might read: “Average Q3 2021 office spending by party (N=218 D, 212 R). Error bars show 95% CI. Source: ProPublica disbursement data.”
For the constituent, simplify to a single comparison with plain language. The caption might read: “Your representative’s office spent $387,000 last quarter, compared to the average of $340,000 across all congressional offices. Most of this spending went to staff salaries and office rent.”









NoteExercise 2: Lead with the finding





Take any analysis you have done so far in this book (loading Census data, aggregating CDC mortality data, visualizing weather distributions). Write a three-sentence summary that leads with the finding, provides one supporting number, and briefly notes a caveat.
Solution:
Example: “Heart disease deaths in the U.S. increased 5.4% from 2019 to 2020, the largest year-over-year jump in the past decade. Total deaths rose from approximately 657,000 to 692,000, a change that coincides with the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. This increase likely reflects both direct cardiovascular effects of COVID-19 and disruptions to routine cardiac care, though the CDC data does not distinguish between these mechanisms.”









NoteExercise 3: Audience analysis





Identify three different audiences for the county-level COVID data you will encounter in Chapter 12 (e.g., a public health official, a journalist, a concerned citizen). For each, write one sentence describing what they already know, what they need to decide, and what chart type would serve them best.
Solution:
A public health official knows epidemiological terminology and needs to decide where to allocate resources. A choropleth map of per-capita case rates, colored by severity thresholds, would serve them best because it shows geographic hotspots at a glance.
A journalist knows the broad pandemic narrative and needs to decide whether a local angle is newsworthy. A comparative bar chart showing the local county against state and national averages would work because it immediately establishes whether the local situation is typical or exceptional.
A concerned citizen has limited statistical background and needs to decide whether to change personal behavior. A simple line chart of local weekly trends with a plain-language annotation (“cases rising” or “cases stable”) and no jargon would be most effective.









NoteExercise 4: Choose a narrative structure





You have been asked to create a data story about congressional spending for a class blog. Describe how the story would differ if you used the Martini Glass structure versus the Drill-Down structure. Which would you choose for your classmates? Why?
Solution:
The Martini Glass would begin with the headline finding (“Congressional offices spend an average of $340,000 per quarter, but the range is enormous”) and a single chart, then offer an interactive table or dashboard where readers can look up their own representative. This works well for classmates who may not read past the first paragraph.
The Drill-Down would start with total spending, then break down by party, then by spending category, then highlight a few interesting outliers. This builds understanding progressively but requires the reader to stay engaged through multiple charts. For classmates in a data science course, the drill-down may be more appropriate because the audience is learning analytical techniques, not just consuming findings.









NoteExercise 5: Redundancy in visualization





Take the DIA passenger chart and encode the same information in at least two visual channels (e.g., both color and a text annotation, or both line thickness and a legend label). How does the redundancy affect readability?
Solution:

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

passenger_df['Grand'].plot(ax=ax, color='steelblue', linewidth=2)

# Redundancy: both a vertical line AND a text annotation for the same event
ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-01'), color='crimson', linestyle='--',
           linewidth=1.5, label='COVID-19 Onset')
ax.annotate('COVID-19\nOnset', xy=(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-01'), 5_000_000),
            fontsize=10, color='crimson', fontweight='bold')

ax.legend()
ax.set_ylabel('Monthly Passengers')
ax.set_title('DIA Passenger Traffic')

The redundancy ensures that a reader who misses the vertical line will still see the annotation, and vice versa. This is especially helpful when charts are printed in black and white or viewed by readers with color vision deficiencies.

title: 8  Writing for an Audience
text:
8.9 Looking Ahead
You now have a framework for thinking about audience that will inform every visualization and data narrative you produce. In Chapter 9, we shift to a new data type — temporal data — and the unique challenges of working with dates, times, and timestamps in Python.

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.1 Setup
Dates and times are among the most common — and most frustrating — data types you will encounter. Sixty seconds in a minute, sixty minutes in an hour, twenty-four hours in a day, seven days in a week, and then months of varying lengths: 28, 29, 30, or 31 days depending on the month and whether it is a leap year. Add time zones, daylight saving time, and inconsistent string formatting across countries and systems, and you have a recipe for subtle but consequential errors.
This chapter introduces Python’s datetime library and pandas’s Timestamp objects. By the end, you will be able to parse dates from strings, compute time differences, extract temporal components, create date ranges, set datetime indices, slice data by date, plot time series, and compute rolling averages. These skills are the foundation for the pattern-finding techniques in Chapter 10.
import numpy as np
np.set_printoptions(suppress=True)

import pandas as pd
idx = pd.IndexSlice
pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import seaborn as sb

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.2 Python’s datetime Library
A “classic” joke among Python programmers is the very justifiable confusion between the datetime object within the datetime module. Time and dates are confusing to work with, so let’s take a moment to embrace the awkwardness.

import datetime


9.2.1 Creating datetime objects
The .now() method gets the current time.

now = datetime.datetime.now()
now

There is also a .utcnow() that gets Coordinated Universal Time — basically the time at 0° longitude in London — rather than your system’s local time.

utc = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
utc

You create a specific datetime by passing year, month, day, and optionally hour, minute, and second as integers.

birthday = datetime.datetime(1984, 2, 13, 7, 30, 15)
birthday







WarningStrings are not datetimes



Passing a string like '2020-09-25' to datetime.datetime() will raise a TypeError. Python’s datetime constructor expects integers, not strings. To convert strings to datetimes, use strptime (described below).



datetime.datetime('2020-09-25')



9.2.2 Attributes and methods
Every datetime object has attributes for its individual components: .year, .month, .day, .hour, .minute, .second.

now.second

Methods for converting to simpler objects:

now.date()


now.time()

Other powerful methods include .weekday() (Monday=0 through Sunday=6), .isoweekday() (Monday=1 through Sunday=7), and .isoformat() for standardized string output.

print(now)


now.isoformat()



9.2.3 strftime and strptime
Dates and times are often stored as strings like “2020-07-04” or “07/04/2020” to represent July 4, 2020 in the U.S. — or April 7, 2020 in Europe. (See? Dates and times can be really frustrating! Check out date format by country.)
If you want to convert a datetime to a string, use strftime (f for format). If you want to convert a string to a datetime, use strptime (p for parse). Both use a shared grammar of format codes documented in strftime and strptime behavior.



Code
Meaning
Example




%Y
4-digit year
2024


%m
2-digit month
03


%d
2-digit day
15


%H
24-hour hour
14


%M
Minute
30


%S
Second
45


%A
Full weekday name
Monday


%B
Full month name
March


%b
Abbreviated month
Mar


%p
AM/PM
PM



Format a datetime as a custom string:

datetime.datetime(2024, 4, 1, 12, 0, 0).strftime("%A! %b %d, %Y and we're at %S seconds")

Parse a string into a datetime:

str_time = datetime.datetime.strptime(
    'Friday, February 29, 9960 11:27 am',
    '%A, %B %d, %Y %H:%M %p'
)
str_time



9.2.4 Unix timestamps
Sometimes computer scientists represent time as the number of seconds elapsed since midnight on January 1, 1970. That moment was second 0, and every second since is incremented. This is popularly known as “Unix time” or the “Unix epoch.”

now.timestamp()

You can convert Unix timestamps back to datetime objects with fromtimestamp.

datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(10000000000)







TipThe Year 2038 Problem



Many of you are too young to remember the panic around the Y2K problem. But there is a much bigger problem known as the Year 2038 problem: 32-bit systems that store time as Unix timestamps will overflow on January 19, 2038 at 3:14:07 UTC when the timestamp exceeds \(2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647\). Mark that date in your calendars.




9.2.5 timedelta
Subtracting one datetime from another produces a timedelta object representing the elapsed time.

birthday_td = now - birthday
birthday_td


birthday_td.seconds

You can add a timedelta to a datetime to compute a future or past date.

birthday + datetime.timedelta(seconds=1)

You can convert timedelta objects into floats by dividing by a “unit” timedelta. This returns your age in days:

birthday_td / datetime.timedelta(hours=24)

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.3 Dates and Times in Pandas
Pandas has its own type called Timestamp for representing dates and times, distinct from datetime. There are good reasons for pandas to have its own representation, but juggling two distinct and sometimes incompatible date representations within Python can definitely be frustrating.

9.3.1 Timestamp
The biggest and most powerful difference between datetime.datetime and pd.Timestamp is that Timestamp will automatically recognize and convert strings to Timestamps without needing the gnarly strftime/strptime syntax.
Note that pandas’s Timestamp is very, very, very different from the Unix time .timestamp() method in datetime.

ts_now = pd.Timestamp('2024-04-17 7:25')
ts_now

Pass a datetime object to pandas’s Timestamp:

ts_birthday = pd.Timestamp(birthday)
ts_birthday

Confirm that these are really different types:

type(birthday)


type(ts_birthday)



9.3.2 Additional attributes and methods
Pandas Timestamp objects have all the attributes of datetime plus several extras: .quarter (what quarter of the year), .is_leap_year (boolean), .day_name(), .to_pydatetime() (convert back to datetime).

ts_birthday.to_pydatetime()


pd.Timestamp('2024-01-13').is_leap_year


ts_birthday.is_leap_year



9.3.3 pd.to_datetime
Just as Timestamp auto-magically converts individual strings, the to_datetime() function can take an array of misshapen and inconsistently typed date representations and convert them all into a uniform array of Timestamp objects.

misshapen_data = [
    'July 4, 2020',
    '9/11/01',
    datetime.datetime(1984, 2, 13, 7),
    datetime.date(2006, 6, 6)
]

pd.to_datetime(misshapen_data, format='mixed')



9.3.4 Parsing dates during CSV loading
When reading a CSV file, you have two options for converting date columns. The first is to pass column names to the parse_dates parameter in read_csv.

sp500_df = pd.read_csv('sp500.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])
sp500_df.head()


sp500_df.loc[0, 'Date']

The second option is to convert after loading with pd.to_datetime.

sp500_df = pd.read_csv('sp500.csv')
sp500_df.loc[0, 'Date']  # Still a string


sp500_df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(sp500_df['Date'])
sp500_df.loc[0, 'Date']  # Now a Timestamp

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.4 date_range
The date_range function creates an array of evenly spaced dates. Key parameters: start, end, freq (defaulting to daily), and periods.

pd.date_range(start='March 1, 2022', end='March 31, 2024', freq='D')


pd.date_range(start='2022-03-01', end='2022-03-31', freq='H')

Create 12 hourly timestamps starting now:

pd.date_range(start='2024-03-07 00:00', periods=12, freq='H')

Create only business days (look up frequency aliases):

pd.date_range(start='2020-01-01', end='2024-03-07', freq='B')







NoteExercise 1: Quarter starts since 2000





Create a date_range of quarter starts from January 1, 2000 to today. How many quarters is that?
Solution:

quarters = pd.date_range(start='2000-01-01', end='2024-10-17', freq='QS')
print(f"{len(quarters)} quarters")
quarters

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
section: 9.5 Indexing and Slicing with Datetime Indices
text:
9.5 Indexing and Slicing with Datetime Indices
We often use the Timestamp column as an index, which enables powerful date-based slicing.

sp500_df.set_index('Date', inplace=True)
sp500_df.head()

Access a specific date — you can use several different string formats:

sp500_df.loc['2020 Mar 23']


sp500_df.loc['March 23, 2020']


sp500_df.loc['2020-03-23']


sp500_df.loc[pd.Timestamp('2020-03-23')]

Slice a date range using strings:

sp500_df.loc['2020-03-01':'2020-03-31', 'Adj Close']

Slice an entire year with just the year string:

sp500_df.loc['2020', 'Adj Close']

You can also slice with datetime and Timestamp objects:

sp500_df.loc[datetime.datetime(2020, 3, 1):datetime.datetime(2020, 3, 31), 'Adj Close']







NoteExercise 2: Load and slice weather data





Load the Boulder weather data with dates parsed. Set the “DATE” column as the index. Slice to just 2020 data.
Solution:

weather_df = pd.read_csv('boulder_weather.csv', sep='|', parse_dates=['DATE'])
weather_df.set_index('DATE', inplace=True)

weather_df.loc['2020']

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.6 Plotting Temporal Data
With a datetime index, pandas handles x-axis formatting automatically.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

sp500_df.loc['2020':, 'Adj Close'].plot(ax=ax)

ax.set_title('S&P500 Adjusted Closing Values, 2020–present')
ax.set_ylabel('Adjusted Close')
ax.set_xlabel('Date')

If the x-axis labels overlap, use f.autofmt_xdate() to rotate them.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

sp500_df.loc['2022':, 'Adj Close'].plot(ax=ax)

ax.set_title('S&P500 Adjusted Closing Values, 2022–present')
ax.set_ylabel('Adjusted Close')

f.autofmt_xdate(rotation=0, ha='center')







NoteExercise 3: Plot the weather data for 2019





Plot the daily temperature (“TEMP”) from the Boulder weather data for the year 2019. Label your axes.
Solution:

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))

weather_df.loc['2019', 'TEMP'].plot(ax=ax)

ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°F)')
ax.set_title('Daily Temperatures in Boulder, CO (2019)')

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.7 Rolling Averages
A rolling average smooths out noisy data by averaging observations over a sliding window. You chain .rolling() with an aggregation method like .mean(), .median(), or .max().

sp500_2020 = pd.DataFrame()

sp500_2020['Raw'] = sp500_df.loc[:, 'Adj Close']
sp500_2020['7-day'] = sp500_2020['Raw'].rolling(7).mean()
sp500_2020['90-day'] = sp500_2020['Raw'].rolling(90).mean()
sp500_2020['365-day'] = sp500_2020['Raw'].rolling(365).mean()

sp500_2020.head(10)


f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

sp500_2020['Raw'].plot(ax=ax, lw=1, c='tab:blue', label='Raw')
sp500_2020['7-day'].plot(ax=ax, lw=2, c='tab:orange', label='7-day')
sp500_2020['90-day'].plot(ax=ax, lw=2, c='tab:green', label='90-day')
sp500_2020['365-day'].plot(ax=ax, lw=3, c='tab:red', label='365-day')

ax.legend(loc='upper left')
ax.set_ylabel('Adjusted Close ($)')
ax.set_title('S&P 500 with Rolling Averages')







TipChoosing window size



A short window (7 days) preserves detail but remains noisy. A long window (365 days) reveals long-term trends but lags behind recent changes. Choose a window based on the time scale of the pattern you want to reveal.








NoteExercise 4: Rolling average of temperatures





Plot a 30-day rolling average of Boulder temperatures since 2018. How does it compare to the raw daily data?
Solution:

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))

weather_df.loc['2018':, 'TEMP'].plot(ax=ax, alpha=0.3, label='Daily')
weather_df.loc['2018':, 'TEMP'].rolling(30).mean().plot(ax=ax, lw=2, label='30-day avg')

ax.legend()
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°F)')
ax.set_title('Boulder Daily Temperatures with 30-Day Rolling Average')

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.8 Reindexing
Temporal data often has gaps — the stock market is closed on weekends and holidays, but social media data is continuous. Comparing both requires a common index. The .reindex() method updates a DataFrame’s index to match a new one, filling gaps with NaN.

sp500_df.index.min(), sp500_df.index.max()


sp500_daterange = pd.date_range(
    start=sp500_df.index.min(),
    end=sp500_df.index.max(),
    freq='D'
)

print(f"Trading days: {sp500_df.index.size}, Calendar days: {sp500_daterange.size}")


sp500_reindexed_df = sp500_df.reindex(sp500_daterange)
print(sp500_reindexed_df.shape)
sp500_reindexed_df.head(10)

The NaN values on Saturdays and Sundays are visible. You could fill them with .fillna(method='ffill') (forward-fill the previous trading day’s value) depending on your analytical needs.

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.9 Grouping Temporal Data
You can use pd.Grouper inside .groupby() to aggregate time series to a lower frequency. Here we compute the total monthly trading volume.

sp500_df.groupby(
    pd.Grouper(freq='MS')
).agg({'Volume': 'sum'}).plot()







NoteExercise 5: Monthly average wind speed





Group the Boulder weather data by month using pd.Grouper(freq='MS') and compute the average wind speed (“WDSP”). Plot the result.
Solution:

monthly_wind = weather_df.groupby(pd.Grouper(freq='MS')).agg({'WDSP': 'mean'})

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
monthly_wind.plot(ax=ax, legend=False)
ax.set_ylabel('Wind Speed (mph)')
ax.set_title('Monthly Average Wind Speed in Boulder')










NoteExercise 6: Parse a custom date format





Given the string "13-Feb-1984 07:30:15", use datetime.datetime.strptime to parse it. Then convert it to a pandas Timestamp.
Solution:

dt = datetime.datetime.strptime("13-Feb-1984 07:30:15", "%d-%b-%Y %H:%M:%S")
ts = pd.Timestamp(dt)
print(ts)










NoteExercise 7: Compute your age





Create a datetime object for your birthday. Compute a timedelta representing your current age. Express your age in days, hours, and approximate years.
Solution:

my_birthday = datetime.datetime(2002, 6, 15)
age = datetime.datetime.now() - my_birthday

print(f"Days: {age.days:,}")
print(f"Hours: {age.days * 24 + age.seconds // 3600:,}")
print(f"Years: {age / datetime.timedelta(days=365.25):.1f}")

title: 9  Working with Temporal Data
text:
9.10 Looking Ahead
You now have the tools to parse, manipulate, and index temporal data in Python. In Chapter 10, you will put these skills to work finding common temporal patterns — hourly rhythms, weekly cycles, annual seasonality — and learning techniques for decomposing time series into their component signals.

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.1 Setup
In Chapter 9 you learned to parse, index, and slice temporal data. This chapter puts those skills to work on a core challenge of data analysis: making sense of noisy temporal data. Raw time series are often too volatile to interpret — daily temperatures swing wildly, stock prices jitter, and taxi pickups spike and plummet. The signal is buried in the noise.
Temporal data is rarely random and uncorrelated. There are often consistent patterns in human and natural behavior that recur at various frequencies: hourly circadian rhythms, weekly work-weekend cycles, monthly billing cycles, and annual seasonality. Identifying and understanding these underlying temporal patterns is crucial for describing behavior, characterizing anomalies, and forecasting future behavior.
This chapter introduces techniques for revealing these patterns: resampling, rolling averages, boxplot summaries, temporal pattern decomposition across frequencies, and formal time series decomposition.
import numpy as np
np.set_printoptions(suppress=True)

import pandas as pd
idx = pd.IndexSlice
pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import ticker

import seaborn as sb
# Define a weekday order — otherwise many libraries will list them alphabetically
weekday_order = ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday', 'Sunday']
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.2 Loading Multiple Datasets
We will work with several datasets at different temporal frequencies to illustrate how the same techniques apply across scales.



Dataset
Frequency




NYC taxi pickups
minute


S&P 500
daily


Wikipedia pageviews
daily


Berkeley Earth temperature
daily


DIA passenger traffic
monthly




10.2.1 NYC taxi pickups
New York City’s Taxi and Limousine Commission publishes high-resolution anonymized data about every trip starting or ending in the city.

nyc_df = pd.read_csv('nyc_pickups.csv', parse_dates=['timestamp'])
nyc_df.set_index('timestamp', inplace=True)

# Extract temporal components for grouping
nyc_df['min'] = nyc_df.index.minute
nyc_df['hour'] = nyc_df.index.hour
nyc_df['weekday'] = nyc_df.index.day_name()

nyc_df.head()



10.2.2 S&P 500

sp500_df = pd.read_csv('sp500.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])
sp500_df.set_index('Date', inplace=True)

sp500_df['weekday'] = sp500_df.index.day_name()
sp500_df['day'] = sp500_df.index.day
sp500_df['month'] = sp500_df.index.month

sp500_df.head()



10.2.3 Berkeley Earth Surface Temperature
Daily land surface temperature estimates from 1880 to the present, from Berkeley Earth.

temp_df = pd.read_csv('avg_temp_1880_2024.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])
temp_df.set_index('Date', inplace=True)

temp_df['weekday'] = temp_df.index.day_name()
temp_df['day'] = temp_df.index.day
temp_df['month'] = temp_df.index.month
temp_df['Year'] = temp_df.index.year

temp_df.head()



10.2.4 DIA passenger traffic

passengers_df = pd.read_csv('passengers.csv')
passengers_df['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(passengers_df['Month'])
passengers_df.set_index('Month', inplace=True)

passengers_df['Total'] = passengers_df.sum(axis=1)
passengers_df['month'] = passengers_df.index.month

passengers_df.tail()
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.3 Visualizing Raw Time Series
Start by plotting the raw temperature data. With over 50,000 daily observations spanning 140+ years, the result is dense and hard to interpret.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 6))

temp_df['AvgTemp'].plot(ax=ax)
ax.set_ylim((0, 16))
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('Average daily temperature 1880–2024')

Is there a warming trend? Hard to say with this much daily variation. We need to smooth the data.
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.4 Resampling
Resampling changes the frequency of your time series. You can use pd.Grouper inside .groupby() or the .resample() method to aggregate from daily to monthly, annual, or any other frequency.

10.4.1 Sampling specific dates
Try resampling to just July 1 of each year. The temperature on a single day is not a reliable indicator of a warming pattern.

july_firsts = pd.date_range(
    start='1880-07-01',
    end='2022-07-01',
    freq=pd.DateOffset(years=1)
)

july_first_temps = temp_df.loc[july_firsts]

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 6))
july_first_temps['AvgTemp'].plot(ax=ax)
ax.set_ylim((6, 12))
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('July 1 temperatures 1880–2022')
ax.axhline(8.59, c='k', ls='--', lw=.5)



10.4.2 Monthly averages
Averaging all days in each month is more robust than sampling a single day.

monthly_avg_temps = temp_df.groupby(pd.Grouper(freq='MS')).agg({'AvgTemp': 'mean'})

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 6))
monthly_avg_temps['AvgTemp'].plot(ax=ax)
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('Average monthly temperature 1880–2024')
ax.axhline(8.59, c='k', ls='--', lw=.5)
ax.set_ylim((6, 12))



10.4.3 Annual averages
Aggregating to annual averages removes the seasonal cycle entirely and reveals the long-term trend.

annual_avg_temps = temp_df.groupby(pd.Grouper(freq='YS')).agg({'AvgTemp': 'mean'})

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 6))
annual_avg_temps['AvgTemp'].plot(ax=ax)
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('Average annual temperature 1880–2024')
ax.axhline(8.59, c='k', ls='--', lw=.5)
ax.set_ylim((6, 12))

The warming trend that was invisible in the daily data is now unmistakable. This illustrates a fundamental trade-off: smoothing reveals trends but removes detail.
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.5 Rolling Averages
Unlike resampling, which bins data into discrete periods, rolling averages produce a value for every data point, making them useful for overlaying on the original data.

quarterly_rollmean = temp_df['AvgTemp'].rolling(90).mean()
annual_rollmean = temp_df['AvgTemp'].rolling(365).mean()
decadal_rollmean = temp_df['AvgTemp'].rolling(3650).mean()


f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 6))

quarterly_rollmean.plot(ax=ax, color='tab:blue', label='Quarterly')
annual_rollmean.plot(ax=ax, color='tab:orange', lw=2, label='Annual')
decadal_rollmean.plot(ax=ax, color='tab:green', lw=5, label='Decadal')

ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('Average daily temperature 1880–2024')
ax.legend(loc='upper left')
ax.axhline(8.59, c='k', ls='--', lw=.5)
ax.set_ylim((6, 12))







NoteExercise 1: Visualize the NYC data





Use nyc_df to visualize the “yellowcab” and “rideshare” columns. Try hourly aggregation, daily aggregation, and overlaying both series.
Solution:

nyc_df.groupby(pd.Grouper(freq='h')).agg({'yellowcab': 'sum'}).plot()


nyc_df[['yellowcab', 'rideshare']].plot(alpha=.5)
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.6 Box Plots for Temporal Distributions
Box plots, introduced in Chapter 7, are excellent for summarizing the distribution of values within each time period. We can create one box per year to show both central tendency and variability.
First, a histogram for a single year (1984):

temps_1984 = temp_df.loc['1984', 'AvgTemp']

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
temps_1984.hist(bins=np.linspace(5, 12, 30), ax=ax, grid=False)
ax.set_xlabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_ylabel('Count (days)')
ax.set_xlim((5, 12))
ax.axvline(temps_1984.mean(), color='tab:red')
ax.axvline(temps_1984.median(), color='tab:orange')

The same data as a box plot:

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(2, 6))
sb.boxplot(y=temps_1984, ax=ax, color='tab:blue', whis=2)
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_ylim((5, 12))

Now a box plot for every year since 1970. The gradual upward shift of the boxes makes the warming trend visible while preserving information about annual variability.

temps_since_1970 = temp_df.loc['1970':, :]

g = sb.catplot(
    data=temps_since_1970,
    x='Year',
    y='AvgTemp',
    kind='box',
    color='tab:blue',
    aspect=3
)

ax = g.axes.flat[0]
ax.axhline(8.59, c='tab:red', ls='--', lw=2, zorder=1)







NoteExercise 2: DIA traffic boxplot





Use passengers_df and seaborn to make a boxplot of the distribution of monthly passenger totals (y-axis) by year (x-axis).
Solution:

passengers_df['Year'] = passengers_df.index.year

g = sb.catplot(
    data=passengers_df,
    x='Year',
    y='Total',
    kind='box',
    aspect=2.5,
    color='steelblue'
)

g.set_xticklabels(rotation=45)
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.7 Temporal Patterns at Different Frequencies
One of the most useful applications of temporal data analysis is identifying recurring patterns at different time scales. Let’s systematically examine patterns at the hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, and annual level.

10.7.1 Hourly patterns: Circadian rhythms
Most people in a given location sleep at roughly the same time. When people are asleep, activity drops, and this should be visible in the data.

sb.catplot(
    x='hour',
    y='yellowcab',
    data=nyc_df,
    kind='point',
    aspect=2
)

Taxi pickups are lowest around 4–5 AM and peak during the evening rush.


10.7.2 Weekly patterns: Workdays vs. weekends
We can make a heatmap to show the interaction between hour of day and day of week.

rideshare_pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    nyc_df,
    index='hour',
    columns='weekday',
    values='yellowcab',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

rideshare_pivot = rideshare_pivot.loc[:, weekday_order]

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 6))
sb.heatmap(rideshare_pivot, ax=ax, square=True)
f.tight_layout()

The heatmap reveals that weekday patterns (morning and evening peaks) differ from weekend patterns (later starts, more nightlife activity). Different datasets show different weekly patterns.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
sb.pointplot(x='weekday', y='Volume', data=sp500_df, order=weekday_order)


f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
sb.pointplot(x='weekday', y='AvgTemp', data=temp_df, order=weekday_order)







TipThe weekday temperature “pattern”



If you see a tiny weekly pattern in global temperature data, pause before concluding that the weather follows a weekly cycle. Any apparent pattern is likely an artifact of the data processing, measurement timing, or station distribution — not a real physical phenomenon. Critical thinking about spurious patterns is essential.




10.7.3 Day-of-month patterns

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
sb.pointplot(x='day', y='Volume', data=sp500_df)



10.7.4 Annual patterns: Seasonality
Seasonality is one of the most common temporal patterns. Temperature has an obvious annual cycle, but many human activities do as well.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
sb.pointplot(x='month', y='AvgTemp', data=temp_df)


f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
sb.pointplot(x='month', y='Total', data=passengers_df)

DIA passenger traffic shows a strong seasonal pattern: summer months (June–August) are busiest, with a secondary peak in March (spring break).
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.8 Decomposing Time Series
A more formal approach to separating trend, seasonal, and residual components uses statistical decomposition. The statsmodels library provides seasonal_decompose, which breaks a time series into three additive (or multiplicative) components: trend, seasonal pattern, and residual noise.

import statsmodels.api as sm


f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
passengers_df['Total'].plot(ax=ax)
ax.set_ylabel('Total passengers')
ax.set_title('Monthly passenger traffic through DIA')

A 12-month rolling average removes the seasonal signal to reveal the trend.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

passengers_df['Total'].plot(ax=ax)
passengers_df['Total'].rolling(12).mean().plot(ax=ax, c='tab:green', lw=2)

ax.set_ylabel('Total passengers')
ax.set_title('Monthly DIA traffic with 12-month rolling average')


10.8.1 Formal decomposition
The seasonal_decompose function produces four panels: the observed data, the estimated trend, the seasonal component, and the residual.

decomposition = sm.tsa.seasonal_decompose(passengers_df['Total'], model='additive')

plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (8, 8)
f = decomposition.plot()

for _ax in f.axes:
    _ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2001-09-11'), color='r', ls='--', lw=1, zorder=0)
    _ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2020-03-11'), color='r', ls='--', lw=1, zorder=0)

The trend component shows steady growth interrupted by 9/11 and COVID-19. The seasonal component shows the consistent summer peak and winter trough. The residual captures everything the trend and seasonal components do not explain — including the massive COVID shock.


10.8.2 Linear modeling of trend
You can also model the long-term trend with a linear regression, as introduced in Chapter 13. First, create a numeric feature for months since the airport opened.

passengers_df['months_since_opening'] = (
    passengers_df.index - pd.Timestamp('1995-01-01')
) / pd.Timedelta(30, 'D')

g = sb.lmplot(
    x='months_since_opening',
    y='Total',
    data=passengers_df,
    aspect=2,
    ci=0,
    scatter_kws={'color': 'k', 's': 25},
    line_kws={'lw': 5, 'color': 'r', 'zorder': 0}
)

The raw data has enormous variance because of seasonality. Smoothing first with a 12-month rolling average and then fitting a linear model produces a much cleaner trend estimate.

passengers_df['Total_smoothed'] = passengers_df['Total'].rolling(12).mean()

g = sb.lmplot(
    x='months_since_opening',
    y='Total_smoothed',
    data=passengers_df,
    aspect=2,
    ci=0,
    scatter_kws={'color': 'k', 's': 25},
    line_kws={'lw': 5, 'color': 'r', 'zorder': 0}
)







NoteExercise 3: Rolling averages on NYC data





Compute 7-day and 30-day rolling averages of daily yellow cab pickups. Plot them together. Which window size better reveals the weekly cycle? Which better reveals the monthly trend?
Solution:

daily_cabs = nyc_df.groupby(pd.Grouper(freq='D')).agg({'yellowcab': 'sum'})

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
daily_cabs['yellowcab'].rolling(7).mean().plot(ax=ax, label='7-day', alpha=0.7)
daily_cabs['yellowcab'].rolling(30).mean().plot(ax=ax, label='30-day', alpha=0.9)
ax.legend()
ax.set_ylabel('Daily Pickups')
ax.set_title('Rolling Average Yellow Cab Pickups')

The 7-day average smooths daily variation while preserving weekly structure. The 30-day average smooths weeks and reveals monthly-scale trends.









NoteExercise 4: Year-over-year DIA comparison





Compute the annual sum of DIA passengers. Then compute the year-over-year percentage change. Which year had the biggest decline? The biggest recovery?
Solution:

annual = passengers_df.groupby(passengers_df.index.year).agg({'Total': 'sum'})
annual['pct_change'] = annual['Total'].pct_change() * 100
annual

2020 will show the largest decline (COVID-19); 2021 will show the largest recovery.









NoteExercise 5: Seasonal overlay





Create a line plot with monthly total passengers for each year overlaid on the same axes (one line per year). Do you see a consistent seasonal pattern?
Solution:

passengers_df['Year'] = passengers_df.index.year
passengers_df['MonthNum'] = passengers_df.index.month

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

for year, group in passengers_df.groupby('Year'):
    ax.plot(group['MonthNum'], group['Total'], alpha=0.4)

ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Total Passengers')
ax.set_title('DIA Monthly Passengers by Year (overlaid)')
ax.set_xticks(range(1, 13))

The seasonal pattern is highly consistent: summer peaks, winter troughs, March secondary peak. The 2020 outlier year is clearly visible as the line that collapses in March.









NoteExercise 6: Heatmap of Wikipedia pageviews





If you have access to Wikipedia pageview data, create a heatmap showing pageviews by hour and day of week. What patterns do you see? How do they differ from the taxi data?
Solution:
Wikipedia pageviews would likely show higher traffic during work hours (people researching at their desks) and a weekly pattern with lower traffic on weekends. Unlike taxi pickups, which peak during commuting hours, Wikipedia traffic may peak during the mid-afternoon “procrastination window.”
