title: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns
text:
10.9 Looking Ahead
You have developed a toolkit for visualizing and analyzing temporal data: resampling at different frequencies, smoothing with rolling averages, decomposing into trend and seasonal components, and identifying patterns across time scales. In Chapter 11, you will address one of the most important and most neglected aspects of data communication: how to honestly represent what you do not know.
crumbs: 10  Time Series Visualization and Patterns

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.1 Setup
The previous chapters treated data as known and reliable. In practice, every dataset carries uncertainty: sampling error, measurement noise, missing observations, model assumptions, and the inherent unpredictability of the phenomena being studied. Communicating uncertainty honestly is one of the most important responsibilities you have as a data analyst, and one of the most commonly neglected.
This chapter covers the sources of uncertainty in data analysis, visual techniques for representing it, and strategies for communicating it to audiences who would prefer neat, definitive answers. These themes draw on research by Wilke (Fundamentals of Data Visualization), Padilla and colleagues on hazard uncertainty visualization, Gustafson and Rice on uncertainty in science communication, and Spiegelhalter on risk communication.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sb
from scipy import stats

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.2 Sources of Uncertainty
Uncertainty enters your analysis at multiple stages, and each source requires different communication strategies.
Sampling uncertainty. Most datasets are samples from a larger population. The American Community Survey data you used in Chapter 1 is a survey of roughly 3.5 million households out of 130 million — an excellent sample, but a sample nonetheless. Summary statistics computed from the sample (means, medians, percentages) are estimates, and they come with margins of error. The Census data even included these margins of error explicitly, which is why each variable appeared as a triplet of estimate, margin of error, and percentage.
Measurement uncertainty. Temperature sensors have precision limits. Survey respondents misremember, misunderstand, or decline to answer honestly. COVID-19 case counts depend on testing rates, which varied enormously across time, geography, and policy. The number you see in your DataFrame is not necessarily the true value — it is a measurement, and all measurements have error.
Model uncertainty. When you fit a rolling average in Chapter 10 or a linear regression in Chapter 13, the estimated parameters are uncertain. Different samples from the same population would produce different estimates. The confidence interval around a regression line represents this model uncertainty.
Missing data. As you saw in Chapter 1, missing data is common and its absence is rarely random. Counties with few COVID cases may be suppressed from CDC data for privacy reasons. This systematically biases any aggregate statistics you compute.
Temporal uncertainty. In Chapter 10 you used rolling averages to smooth noisy data. The window size you chose affected the result: a 7-day window and a 365-day window tell different stories from the same data. Neither is wrong, but both embed assumptions about the relevant time scale of the pattern you care about.

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.3 Visualizing Uncertainty

11.3.1 Error bars
The simplest way to represent uncertainty is with error bars. Seaborn’s bar and point plots include 95% confidence intervals by default — those black lines you saw at the top of the bars in Chapter 7.

deaths_df = pd.read_csv('CDC_deaths.csv', parse_dates=['Week date'])

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
sb.barplot(
    data=deaths_df,
    x='Year',
    y='Diseases of heart',
    errorbar='ci',
    color='indianred',
    ax=ax
)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly Heart Disease Deaths')
ax.set_title('Average Weekly Heart Disease Deaths by Year (with 95% CI)')

The black lines represent the 95% confidence interval: we can be 95% confident that the true mean falls within this range. Wider intervals indicate more variability in the underlying data. Notice that the 2020 bar has a wider interval — the pandemic introduced more week-to-week variation in heart disease mortality than previous years.


11.3.2 Confidence bands on time series
For time series and regression lines, uncertainty is typically shown as a shaded band around the central estimate. You can construct this from the mean and standard deviation of aggregated data.

temp_df = pd.read_csv('avg_temp_1880_2024.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])
temp_df.set_index('Date', inplace=True)

annual = temp_df.groupby(pd.Grouper(freq='YS')).agg({'AvgTemp': ['mean', 'std']})
annual.columns = ['mean', 'std']

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
ax.plot(annual.index, annual['mean'], color='tab:red', linewidth=1.5)
ax.fill_between(
    annual.index,
    annual['mean'] - 2 * annual['std'],
    annual['mean'] + 2 * annual['std'],
    alpha=0.2,
    color='tab:red'
)
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('Annual Average Surface Temperature with ±2σ Band')

The shaded band shows ±2 standard deviations around the annual mean, capturing about 95% of the daily variation within each year. Notice that the band width is relatively stable: the warming trend is in the central tendency, not in the variability. This is itself an important finding — the world is not just warming on average, it is warming consistently.


11.3.3 Box plots as uncertainty summaries
Box plots, introduced in Chapter 7 and used extensively in Chapter 10, are another way to communicate distributional uncertainty. They show the median, interquartile range, and outliers without assuming a specific distribution shape.


11.3.4 Hypothetical outcome plots
A more advanced technique involves showing multiple plausible outcomes rather than a single summary. Instead of one regression line with a confidence band, you can draw multiple regression lines sampled from the distribution of possible models. This “ensemble” approach, sometimes called a hypothetical outcome plot, helps audiences develop an intuitive sense of model uncertainty.

np.random.seed(42)
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 50)
true_slope, true_intercept = 2.5, 1.0

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

# Draw 20 plausible regression lines from noisy data
for _ in range(20):
    noise = np.random.normal(0, 3, len(x))
    y = true_slope * x + true_intercept + noise
    slope = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
    ax.plot(x, np.polyval(slope, x), color='steelblue', alpha=0.15)

# Draw the "true" line
ax.plot(x, true_slope * x + true_intercept, color='red', linewidth=2,
        label='True relationship')
ax.legend()
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.set_title('Hypothetical Outcome Plot: 20 Plausible Regression Lines')

Each semi-transparent blue line represents one plausible regression from one noisy sample. The spread of lines communicates the uncertainty more intuitively than a single confidence band.

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.4 Communicating Uncertainty in Writing
Visualizing uncertainty is only half the challenge. The other half is writing about it in a way that is honest without being paralyzing.

11.4.1 Use calibrated language
Match the strength of your language to the strength of the evidence. “The data shows” implies near-certainty. “The data suggests” implies strong evidence with some uncertainty. “The data is consistent with” implies that other explanations are also plausible. Each phrasing makes a different epistemic commitment, and you should choose deliberately.


11.4.2 Provide context for numbers
A margin of error of ±3 percentage points means very different things depending on the estimate. An approval rating of 51% ± 3% could be a majority or a minority — the uncertainty is substantively important. An approval rating of 75% ± 3% is clearly a majority — the uncertainty barely matters. Always report the interval alongside the point estimate, and help the reader understand whether the uncertainty affects the conclusion.


11.4.3 Acknowledge what you don’t know
If your county-level COVID analysis is missing data from counties with small populations, say so. If your regression model explains only 15% of the variation, say so. If your rolling average window was chosen for convenience rather than theory, say so. Readers who discover these limitations on their own will lose trust. Readers who hear them from you will gain it.


11.4.4 Avoid false precision
Reporting a temperature trend to four decimal places implies a level of precision that the underlying data does not support. Round to a meaningful number of significant figures. If your data has daily variability of ±2°C, reporting a trend of 0.0173°C per year creates a misleading impression of precision.






WarningThe certainty trap



Audiences — especially decision-makers — prefer certainty. They want to hear “X will happen” rather than “X will probably happen, but there is a 30% chance of Y.” Resist the temptation to overstate your confidence. An honest assessment of uncertainty is more useful and more ethical than a confident-but-wrong prediction. As Pearce writes in The Economist’s guide to uncertainty visualization, “Showing the range of possibilities helps people make better decisions than giving them a single number and hoping they treat it appropriately.”

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.5 Uncertainty and Decision-Making
How you present uncertainty should depend on the stakes of the decision it informs, connecting back to the audience analysis framework from Chapter 8.
For low-stakes decisions (what restaurant to visit, which article to read next), a simple recommendation is fine. The cost of being wrong is small, and a detailed uncertainty analysis would be more burdensome than helpful.
For high-stakes decisions (should a city invest in a new transit line, should a hospital change its staffing model), presenting the full range of plausible outcomes is essential. A forecast that shows both the best-case and worst-case scenarios helps decision-makers plan for contingencies. This is where confidence intervals, scenario analysis, and hypothetical outcome plots earn their keep.
For public communication (a newspaper article about climate trends, a blog post about pandemic data), the challenge is conveying uncertainty without undermining the core finding. The phrase “scientists are 95% confident that global temperatures have increased” is more accessible and equally honest as reporting a p-value of 0.001. Translate technical uncertainty measures into language your audience can interpret.

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.6 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Add uncertainty to a chart





Take any bar chart you produced in Chapter 7 and ensure it includes error bars. Then write a one-sentence interpretation of what the error bars tell you about the reliability of the comparison between categories.
Solution:
Seaborn includes 95% confidence interval error bars by default in catplot with kind='bar'. The key interpretation: if the error bars for two categories overlap substantially, the observed difference may not be statistically significant. If they do not overlap, the difference is likely real — though overlap alone is not a definitive test. Only a proper statistical test (like the t-tests from Chapter 7) can establish significance.









NoteExercise 2: Compare point estimates and intervals





Using the CDC mortality data, compute the mean and 95% confidence interval for weekly cancer deaths in 2019 and 2020. Print both. Write a sentence interpreting whether the difference is meaningful.
Solution:

deaths_df = pd.read_csv('CDC_deaths.csv', parse_dates=['Week date'])

for year in [2019, 2020]:
    data = deaths_df.loc[deaths_df['Year'] == year, 'Malignant neoplasms'].dropna()
    mean = data.mean()
    ci = stats.t.interval(
        0.95, len(data) - 1,
        loc=mean,
        scale=stats.sem(data)
    )
    print(f"{year}: Mean = {mean:.0f}, 95% CI = ({ci[0]:.0f}, {ci[1]:.0f})")

If the confidence intervals do not overlap, the difference in average weekly cancer deaths between 2019 and 2020 is statistically significant. Substantively, consider whether the magnitude of the difference is large enough to reflect a real change in cancer mortality or merely a shift in how deaths were classified during the pandemic.









NoteExercise 3: Rewrite with calibrated language





Take the following overconfident sentence and rewrite it with appropriate uncertainty language:
“Our analysis proves that broadband access causes lower COVID death rates.”
Solution:
“Our analysis shows a statistically significant negative association between broadband access and per-capita COVID death rates at the county level (p < 0.001, R² = 0.12). However, this cross-sectional correlation does not establish causation, and the model explains only 12% of the variation in death rates. Other factors — including urbanization, income, healthcare access, and age distribution — likely contribute to both broadband adoption and health outcomes.”
This rewrite replaces “proves” with “shows an association,” explicitly names the test statistic and effect size, acknowledges the model’s limited explanatory power, and identifies confounders. It is more honest and, paradoxically, more persuasive because it demonstrates the analyst’s awareness of limitations.









NoteExercise 4: Window size sensitivity





Using the Berkeley Earth temperature data, compute 90-day, 365-day, and 3650-day rolling averages. Plot all three on the same axes. Write a paragraph explaining how the choice of window size affects the interpretation of the warming trend. Is one window size “correct”?
Solution:

temp_df = pd.read_csv('avg_temp_1880_2024.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])
temp_df.set_index('Date', inplace=True)

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
temp_df['AvgTemp'].rolling(90).mean().plot(ax=ax, alpha=0.5, label='90-day')
temp_df['AvgTemp'].rolling(365).mean().plot(ax=ax, alpha=0.7, label='365-day')
temp_df['AvgTemp'].rolling(3650).mean().plot(ax=ax, lw=3, label='10-year')
ax.legend()
ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
ax.set_title('Rolling Averages at Different Window Sizes')

The 90-day window preserves seasonal oscillation and year-to-year variation, making the long-term trend difficult to see. The 365-day window removes seasonality and reveals decadal fluctuations. The 10-year window removes almost all short-term variation and shows only the long-term warming trend. No single window size is “correct” — each answers a different question. The 90-day window is useful for identifying individual extreme seasons. The 10-year window is useful for policy discussions about century-scale climate change. The analyst’s responsibility is to be transparent about the choice and its consequences.









NoteExercise 5: Confidence band for DIA traffic





Compute the mean and standard deviation of monthly DIA passenger traffic for each month of the year (January through December) using pre-pandemic data (2015–2019). Plot the monthly mean as a line with a ±1 standard deviation shaded band. Overlay the 2020 and 2021 actual values. Write two sentences interpreting the result.
Solution:

passengers_df = pd.read_csv('passengers.csv')
passengers_df['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(passengers_df['Month'])
passengers_df.set_index('Month', inplace=True)

pre_pandemic = passengers_df.loc['2015':'2019']
monthly_stats = pre_pandemic.groupby(pre_pandemic.index.month).agg(
    {'Grand': ['mean', 'std']}
)
monthly_stats.columns = ['mean', 'std']

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.plot(range(1, 13), monthly_stats['mean'], color='gray', lw=2, label='2015–2019 Mean')
ax.fill_between(
    range(1, 13),
    monthly_stats['mean'] - monthly_stats['std'],
    monthly_stats['mean'] + monthly_stats['std'],
    alpha=0.2, color='gray'
)

# Overlay 2020
actual_2020 = passengers_df.loc['2020', 'Grand']
ax.plot(actual_2020.index.month, actual_2020.values, 'o-',
        color='crimson', label='2020')

ax.set_xticks(range(1, 13))
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Passengers')
ax.set_title('2020 Actual vs. Pre-Pandemic Baseline (±1σ)')
ax.legend()

The 2020 passenger counts fell far below the pre-pandemic confidence band starting in March, reaching levels that are many standard deviations below the historical norm. By late 2020, traffic began recovering but remained well outside the range of normal year-to-year variation, demonstrating that the COVID-19 impact was not a typical fluctuation but a historically unprecedented disruption.

title: 11  Communicating Uncertainty
text:
11.7 Looking Ahead
You now have frameworks for both visualizing and writing about uncertainty. In Chapter 12, you will begin exploring the relationships between pairs of continuous variables — relationships that are always accompanied by uncertainty and always tempt analysts into causal claims that the data alone cannot support.

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.1 Setup
Previous modules have explored the distribution of a single continuous variable (histograms in Chapter 6), the relationship between continuous and categorical variables (catplots in Chapter 7), and time series as a special kind of continuous data (line plots in Chapter 10). This chapter explores the relationship between two continuous variables and its corresponding visualization: the scatterplot.
Scatterplots are deceptively simple — two axes, a cloud of dots — but the choices you make about size, color, transparency, annotation, and axis scales can dramatically affect what the reader sees and concludes.
import numpy as np

import pandas as pd
idx = pd.IndexSlice
pd.options.display.max_columns = 200

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import ticker

import seaborn as sb
from scipy import stats

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.2 The Data
We will work with a dataset of U.S. counties combining demographic, economic, political, and health variables. In the Appendix of the companion repository, you can see how several county-level datasets were joined together. The data includes 2020 Census estimates (source) about social characteristics (DP02 table), economic characteristics (DP03 table), and demographic characteristics (DP05 table). It also includes the share of votes cast for Donald Trump in the 2016 and 2020 elections (source) and cumulative COVID-19 case and death counts from The New York Times (source). See Chapter 15 for full provenance details.

counties_df = pd.read_csv('us_counties.csv', dtype={0: str}, na_values=['-'])
counties_df.rename(columns={'Unnamed: 0': 'FIPS'}, inplace=True)
counties_df.set_index('county_fips', inplace=True)
print(counties_df.shape)
counties_df.head()

Let’s filter to just Colorado’s 64 counties for initial exploration.

co_counties = counties_df.copy()[counties_df['state'] == 'Colorado']

print(co_counties.shape)
co_counties.head(8)


biggest_co_counties = co_counties.sort_values('Population', ascending=False)['county'].head(10).values
biggest_co_counties

It is important to emphasize that counties are units of approximately equal geographic size, not equal population. Most Colorado counties have small populations.

ax = co_counties['Population'].hist(bins=np.linspace(0, 8e5, 25), figsize=(8, 4))
ax.set_ylim((0, 50))
ax.grid(None)
ax.set_xlabel('Population')
ax.set_ylabel('Counties')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(ticker.StrMethodFormatter('{x:,.0f}'))

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.3 Making Scatterplots
There are three common interfaces for creating scatterplots in Python. All produce similar results; choose whichever syntax you find most readable.

12.3.1 matplotlib

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

ax.scatter(
    x=co_counties['Median_Income'],
    y=co_counties['covid_deaths_percapita']
)

ax.set_xlabel('Median Income ($)')
ax.set_ylabel('COVID-19 Deaths per Capita')



12.3.2 pandas

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

co_counties.plot.scatter(
    x='Pct_Broadband',
    y='covid_cases_percapita',
    ax=ax
)



12.3.3 seaborn

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

sb.scatterplot(
    x='Pct_Broadband',
    y='covid_cases_percapita',
    data=co_counties,
    ax=ax
)

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.4 Customizing Scatterplots

12.4.1 Point size
Change the size of all points with the s parameter.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

co_counties.plot.scatter(
    x='Median_Income',
    y='covid_deaths_percapita',
    s=5,
    ax=ax
)

Map a third variable to point size to create a bubble chart. This is useful for representing a quantity like college graduation rates without requiring a third axis.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

co_counties['size'] = co_counties['Pct_College_Grad'] ** 1.5

co_counties.plot.scatter(
    x='Median_Income',
    y='covid_deaths_percapita',
    s='size',
    ax=ax
)



12.4.2 Transparency
When points overlap heavily, setting alpha to a low value (0.1–0.3) reveals the density of the point cloud.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

co_counties.plot.scatter(
    x='Median_Income',
    y='covid_deaths_percapita',
    s='size',
    ax=ax,
    alpha=.5
)



12.4.3 Color
Map a variable to color to add a fourth dimension. The pandas interface uses c for a continuous color variable.

ax = co_counties.plot.scatter(
    x='Population',
    y='trump_2020'
)

ax.set_xscale('log')

With seaborn, use hue for the color variable and size for the size variable.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

sb.scatterplot(
    x='Pct_College_Grad',
    y='Median_Income',
    hue='trump_2020',
    size='covid_deaths',
    palette='coolwarm',
    data=co_counties,
    ax=ax
)



12.4.4 Annotations
For small datasets, labeling individual points provides rich context. Loop through the data and use ax.annotate() to place text near each point.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

co_counties.plot.scatter(
    x='Pct_College_Grad',
    y='Median_Income',
    alpha=.75,
    ax=ax
)

for fips, name in co_counties['county'].to_dict().items():
    if name in biggest_co_counties:
        x = co_counties.loc[fips, 'Pct_College_Grad']
        y = co_counties.loc[fips, 'Median_Income']
        ax.annotate(name, (x, y), fontsize=7, alpha=.8)







NoteExercise 1: Three interfaces





Make a scatterplot of “Pct_Unemployed” (x-axis) vs. “Median_Income” (y-axis) for Colorado counties. Color the points by “Pct_White”. Try all three interfaces (matplotlib, pandas, seaborn) and compare.
Solution:

# matplotlib
f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(
    x=co_counties['Median_Income'],
    y=co_counties['Pct_Unemployed'],
    c=co_counties['Pct_White']
)


# pandas
f, ax = plt.subplots()
co_counties.plot.scatter(x='Median_Income', y='Pct_Unemployed', c='Pct_White', ax=ax)


# seaborn
f, ax = plt.subplots()
sb.scatterplot(data=co_counties, x='Median_Income', y='Pct_Unemployed', hue='Pct_White', ax=ax)

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.5 Conventions for Causal Arguments
Of course correlation is not causation, but whenever you make a scatterplot, you are making an implicit causal argument: one variable is influencing the other. The dominant convention is to place the presumed cause on the x-axis and the presumed effect on the y-axis.
If you are arguing that broadband connectivity influences COVID exposure behavior, broadband goes on the x-axis and COVID cases on the y-axis.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_Broadband', y='covid_cases_percapita', ax=ax, alpha=.25)

If you reverse the axes, you are implicitly arguing the opposite causal direction — that COVID exposure influences broadband connectivity — which is much harder to justify theoretically.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='covid_cases_percapita', y='Pct_Broadband', ax=ax)

Be intentional about this choice. Swapping the axes changes the implicit argument.

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.6 Types of Bivariate Relationships

12.6.1 Positive relationship
Counties where more of the population was born in the same state also tend to have higher high school graduation rates. The Pearson correlation coefficient quantifies this linear association.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_Born_Same_State', y='Pct_HS_Grad', ax=ax)


counties_df.loc[:, ['Pct_HS_Grad', 'Pct_Born_Same_State']].corr()

A correlation of ~0.46 is moderate-to-high for social science data, where relationships are complex and layered.


12.6.2 Negative relationship
Counties with more people on public assistance tend to have lower broadband usage.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_Assistance', y='Pct_Broadband', ax=ax)


counties_df.loc[:, ['Pct_Assistance', 'Pct_Broadband']].corr()



12.6.3 No relationship
There is no obvious linear relationship between percent female and percent Hispanic.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_Female', y='Pct_Hispanic', ax=ax)


counties_df.loc[:, ['Pct_Female', 'Pct_Hispanic']].corr()



12.6.4 Non-linear relationship
Income and poverty follow a curve: poverty drops steeply as income rises from low levels, then levels off at high incomes. A linear model would miss the shape of this relationship.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Median_Income', y='Pct_Family_Poverty', ax=ax)


counties_df.loc[:, ['Median_Income', 'Pct_Family_Poverty']].corr()



12.6.5 Reversals and surprises
Sometimes changing one variable to a similar variable can completely reverse a relationship. Trump 2016 vote share increases with more high school graduates but decreases with more college graduates.

f, axs = plt.subplots(nrows=1, ncols=2, figsize=(12, 6), sharey=True,
                       subplot_kw={'ylim': (0, 100)})

counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_HS_Grad', y='trump_2016', ax=axs[0])
axs[0].set_title('Trump 2016 vote share increases with more HS grads')

counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_College_Grad', y='trump_2016', ax=axs[1])
axs[1].set_title('Trump 2016 vote share decreases with more college grads')

f.tight_layout()


counties_df[['Pct_HS_Grad', 'Pct_College_Grad', 'trump_2016']].corr()

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.7 Pair Plots and Correlograms
With many variables, it helps to see all pairwise relationships at once. Seaborn’s pairplot generates a grid of scatterplots.

select_columns = ['trump_2020', 'covid_cases_percapita', 'Pct_Over_65',
                  'Pct_White', 'Pct_Unemployed', 'Median_Income', 'Pct_English_Only']

sb.pairplot(data=counties_df[select_columns])

Customize to show only the lower triangle and overlay regression lines.

sb.pairplot(
    data=counties_df[select_columns],
    kind='reg',
    corner=True,
    plot_kws={
        'scatter_kws': {'alpha': .1},
        'line_kws': {'color': 'red'}
    }
)

Alternatively, compute the full correlation matrix and display it as a heatmap.

counties_corr = counties_df[select_columns].corr()

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))
sb.heatmap(data=counties_corr, square=True, cmap='viridis', vmin=-1, vmax=1, annot=True, fmt='.2f')

Seaborn’s clustermap rearranges the rows and columns so that variables with similar correlations are grouped together.

sb.clustermap(data=counties_corr, cmap='viridis', vmin=-1, vmax=1)







NoteExercise 2: Explore three relationships





Make three scatterplots using different pairs of variables from counties_df. For each, describe whether the relationship appears positive, negative, non-linear, or nonexistent. Report the correlation coefficient. Label your axes.
Solution:

pairs = [
    ('Pct_Unemployed', 'Median_Income'),
    ('Pct_College_Grad', 'Pct_Broadband'),
    ('Pct_Female', 'Pct_Hispanic')
]

for x, y in pairs:
    r = counties_df[[x, y]].corr().iloc[0, 1]
    print(f"{x} vs {y}: r = {r:.3f}")










NoteExercise 3: Size, color, and transparency





Create a scatterplot of all U.S. counties with Pct_Broadband on the x-axis and covid_deaths_percapita on the y-axis. Map population to point size, Trump 2020 vote share to color, and use transparency to handle overplotting. Write a two-sentence interpretation.
Solution:

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))
scatter = ax.scatter(
    x=counties_df['Pct_Broadband'],
    y=counties_df['covid_deaths_percapita'],
    s=counties_df['Population'] / 5000,
    c=counties_df['trump_2020'],
    cmap='RdBu_r',
    alpha=0.3,
    edgecolor='none'
)
plt.colorbar(scatter, ax=ax, label='Trump 2020 Vote Share')
ax.set_xlabel('% Broadband Access')
ax.set_ylabel('COVID Deaths per Capita')

Counties with lower broadband access tend to have higher per-capita COVID death rates, and these lower-broadband counties also tend to have higher Trump vote shares. However, this cross-sectional pattern cannot establish causation — urbanization, income, and healthcare access all covary with these variables.









NoteExercise 4: Log-scaled axes





Create a scatterplot of Population vs. Trump 2020 vote share with a log-scaled x-axis. Why does the log transformation help?
Solution:

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Population', y='trump_2020', ax=ax, alpha=.15, s=5)
ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlabel('Population (log scale)')
ax.set_ylabel('Trump 2020 Vote Share')

The log scale spreads out the thousands of small counties that are otherwise compressed against the y-axis, making the negative relationship between population and Trump vote share visible.









NoteExercise 5: Correlation heatmap





Select 8–10 variables from the counties data. Compute the correlation matrix and display it as a heatmap with annotations. Which pair of variables has the strongest positive correlation? The strongest negative correlation?
Solution:

cols = ['trump_2020', 'Pct_College_Grad', 'Median_Income', 'Pct_Unemployed',
        'Pct_Broadband', 'Pct_White', 'Pct_Over_65', 'covid_deaths_percapita']

corr = counties_df[cols].corr()

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))
sb.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', vmin=-1, vmax=1,
           square=True, ax=ax)

Examine the annotated values to identify the strongest positive and negative pairs.

title: 12  Scatterplots
text:
12.8 Looking Ahead
Scatterplots show you relationships, but they do not quantify them precisely. In Chapter 13, you will learn to estimate and interpret linear regression models that put a number on the slope, strength, and statistical significance of the relationships you have been visualizing.

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.1 Setup
In Chapter 12 you visualized the relationships between pairs of continuous variables and described them qualitatively. This chapter takes the next step: quantifying those relationships with linear regression models. Rather than saying “education and income appear positively related,” you will be able to say “each additional percentage point of college graduates in a county is associated with a $1,200 increase in median income, and the model explains 35% of the variation.”
We will estimate regression models using three different Python libraries — scipy, scikit-learn, and statsmodels — each of which offers different strengths. We will also use seaborn’s regplot and lmplot functions to combine regression estimation with visualization, examine residuals to diagnose model quality, and introduce multiple regression.
import numpy as np

import pandas as pd
idx = pd.IndexSlice
pd.options.display.max_columns = 200

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import ticker

import seaborn as sb
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.2 The Data
We continue using the county-level dataset from Chapter 12.

counties_df = pd.read_csv('us_counties.csv', dtype={0: str}, na_values=['-'])
counties_df.rename(columns={'Unnamed: 0': 'FIPS'}, inplace=True)
counties_df.set_index('county_fips', inplace=True)
print(counties_df.shape)
counties_df.head()

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.3 The Linear Model
A linear regression estimates the parameters of the equation:
\[y = \alpha + \beta x\]
where \(y\) is the outcome variable, \(x\) is the input variable, \(\beta\) is the slope (how much \(y\) changes for each one-unit increase in \(x\)), and \(\alpha\) is the intercept (the predicted value of \(y\) when \(x = 0\)). The parameters are estimated using a method called ordinary least squares (OLS), which finds the line that minimizes the sum of squared differences between observed and predicted values.

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.4 Estimation with scipy.stats.linregress
The simplest interface is scipy.stats.linregress, which takes two arrays and returns the slope, intercept, correlation coefficient (\(r\)), p-value, and standard error.

f, ax = plt.subplots()

sb.regplot(
    data=counties_df,
    x='Pct_Born_Same_State',
    y='Pct_HS_Grad',
    line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'},
    ax=ax
)

ax.set_xlim((0, 100))


model = stats.linregress(
    x=counties_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].fillna(counties_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].mean()),
    y=counties_df['Pct_HS_Grad'].fillna(counties_df['Pct_HS_Grad'].mean())
)

model


13.4.1 Interpreting the parameters
Each parameter has a substantive meaning.
The slope of approximately 0.37 means that each additional percentage point of a county’s population born in the same state is associated with a 0.37 percentage point increase in high school graduation rates.
The intercept of approximately 58 means that the model predicts a high school graduation rate of 58% for a hypothetical county where 0% of the population was born in the same state.
The r-value can be squared to get \(R^2\), the coefficient of determination — the proportion of variance in \(y\) explained by \(x\).

model.rvalue ** 2

The p-value tests the null hypothesis that the slope is zero (no relationship). A very small p-value indicates strong evidence against this null.






WarningMissing data and regression



linregress does not handle NaN values — it will return NaN for all parameters if any input contains missing data. You must handle missing data before fitting, either by dropping rows with .dropna() or imputing with .fillna(). The choice matters and should be documented.




13.4.2 Visualizing the model
Plot the data with the estimated regression line overlaid.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

counties_df.plot.scatter(x='Pct_Born_Same_State', y='Pct_HS_Grad', ax=ax, s=25)

_x = counties_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].fillna(0)
_y = model.intercept + model.slope * _x
ax.plot(_x, _y, color='tab:red')
ax.set_xlim((0, 100))
ax.set_ylim((0, 60))

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.5 Estimation with scikit-learn
For more complex models, scikit-learn’s LinearRegression uses the fit/predict pattern familiar throughout the machine learning ecosystem.

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

X = counties_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].fillna(
    counties_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].mean()
).values.reshape(-1, 1)

y = counties_df['Pct_HS_Grad'].fillna(
    counties_df['Pct_HS_Grad'].mean()
).values.reshape(-1, 1)

sk_reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)

The model statistics are stored as attributes.

sk_reg.coef_


sk_reg.intercept_


sk_reg.score(X, y)

Note that scikit-learn requires the predictor to be a 2D array (hence .reshape(-1, 1)), even for simple linear regression, because the library is designed for models with multiple predictors.

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.6 Estimation with statsmodels
The statsmodels library provides the most detailed statistical output, including confidence intervals, diagnostic tests, and a familiar summary table.

_cleaned_df = counties_df.copy()
_cleaned_df['Pct_HS_Grad'] = _cleaned_df['Pct_HS_Grad'].fillna(_cleaned_df['Pct_HS_Grad'].mean())
_cleaned_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'] = _cleaned_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].fillna(
    _cleaned_df['Pct_Born_Same_State'].mean()
)

sm_reg = smf.ols('Pct_HS_Grad ~ Pct_Born_Same_State', data=_cleaned_df).fit()

print(sm_reg.summary())

The formula interface (y ~ x) is borrowed from R and is intuitive once you get used to it. The summary table includes everything from linregress plus confidence intervals for each coefficient, F-statistics, and diagnostic measures.

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.7 Visualizing Regressions with Seaborn
Seaborn’s regplot and lmplot combine scatterplots with fitted regression lines and confidence bands in a single call. The shaded band around the regression line represents the 95% confidence interval for the predicted mean — connecting back to the uncertainty principles from Chapter 11.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

sb.regplot(
    data=counties_df,
    x='Pct_Born_Same_State',
    y='Pct_HS_Grad',
    line_kws={'color': 'tab:orange'},
    scatter_kws={'s': 15, 'alpha': .5},
    ax=ax
)

ax.set_xlim((0, 100))
ax.set_ylim((0, 60))


13.7.1 Positive relationship

g = sb.lmplot(
    data=counties_df, x='Pct_Born_Same_State', y='Pct_HS_Grad',
    aspect=2, scatter_kws={'alpha': .5}, line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'}
)



13.7.2 Negative relationship

neg_model = stats.linregress(
    x=counties_df['Pct_Assistance'].fillna(0),
    y=counties_df['Pct_Broadband'].fillna(0)
)
print(f"slope={neg_model.slope:.3f}, R²={neg_model.rvalue**2:.3f}")


g = sb.lmplot(
    data=counties_df, x='Pct_Assistance', y='Pct_Broadband',
    aspect=2, scatter_kws={'alpha': .5}, line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'}
)



13.7.3 No relationship

g = sb.lmplot(
    data=counties_df[['Pct_Female', 'Pct_Hispanic']].fillna(0),
    x='Pct_Female', y='Pct_Hispanic',
    aspect=2, scatter_kws={'alpha': .5}, line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'}
)



13.7.4 Non-linear relationship
Income and poverty follow a curve. A log transformation can linearize the relationship.

g = sb.lmplot(
    data=counties_df[['Median_Income', 'Pct_Family_Poverty']].fillna(0),
    x='Median_Income', y='Pct_Family_Poverty',
    aspect=2, scatter_kws={'alpha': .5}, line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'}
)


counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty_log'] = counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty'].apply(np.log).fillna(0)
counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty_log'].replace({-np.inf: 0}, inplace=True)

g = sb.lmplot(
    data=counties_df, x='Median_Income', y='Pct_Family_Poverty_log',
    aspect=2, scatter_kws={'alpha': .5}, line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'}
)


stats.linregress(
    x=counties_df['Median_Income'].fillna(0),
    y=counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty_log'].fillna(0)
)

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.8 Residuals
Unless you have a very simple mechanistic relationship, there is residual variance — the difference between the model’s prediction and the actual observation. Well-performing models have small residuals; poor models have large ones.

model = stats.linregress(
    x=counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty'].fillna(counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty'].mean()),
    y=counties_df['Pct_Unemployed'].fillna(counties_df['Pct_Unemployed'].mean())
)

_x = counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty'].fillna(counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty'].mean())
_y_pred = model.intercept + model.slope * _x

counties_df['residuals'] = _y_pred - counties_df['Pct_Unemployed'].fillna(
    counties_df['Pct_Unemployed'].mean()
)


13.8.1 Diagnosing model quality
A well-specified regression model should have residuals that are independent, have constant variance, are normally distributed, and have a mean of zero.
Residuals vs. predicted values — look for patterns. A random scatter is good; a funnel or curve is bad.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

sb.regplot(x=_y_pred, y=counties_df['residuals'],
           scatter_kws={'alpha': .5}, line_kws={'color': 'tab:red'}, ax=ax)
ax.axhline(0, c='tab:red', lw=2, ls='--')
ax.set_xlabel('Predictions')
ax.set_ylabel('Residuals')

Histogram of residuals — should be approximately normal and centered on zero.

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

counties_df['residuals'].hist(bins=25, ax=ax)
ax.grid(None)
ax.axvline(counties_df['residuals'].mean(), c='tab:green', lw=2)
ax.axvline(0, c='tab:red', lw=2, ls='--')
ax.set_xlabel('Residual')
ax.set_ylabel('Count')

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.9 Multiple Regression
Often you want to estimate an outcome as a function of more than one input variable. statsmodels provides the most intuitive interface for multiple regression via the formula syntax.

one_X_fit = smf.ols('Pct_HS_Grad ~ Median_Income', data=counties_df.fillna(0)).fit()
print(one_X_fit.summary())


two_X_fit = smf.ols('Pct_HS_Grad ~ Pct_Born_Same_State + Median_Income',
                      data=counties_df.fillna(0)).fit()
print(two_X_fit.summary())

Notice that the coefficient for Median_Income changes when Pct_Born_Same_State is added to the model. This is because the two predictors are correlated, and the multiple regression partitions the explained variance between them. The coefficient in a multiple regression represents the effect of each variable holding the other variables constant.






TipInterpreting multiple regression



In a multiple regression, each coefficient represents the expected change in \(y\) for a one-unit increase in that predictor, holding all other predictors constant. This is different from the simple regression coefficient, which does not control for other variables.








NoteExercise 1: Estimate and interpret





Model a relationship between two variables using any of the three libraries. Report the slope, intercept, and \(R^2\). Interpret the slope in plain language including units.
Solution:

model = stats.linregress(
    x=counties_df['Pct_Unemployed'].fillna(0),
    y=counties_df['Pct_Family_Poverty'].fillna(0)
)

print(f"Slope: {model.slope:.3f}")
print(f"Intercept: {model.intercept:.3f}")
print(f"R²: {model.rvalue**2:.3f}")

Each additional percentage point of unemployment is associated with approximately a 1.5 percentage point increase in family poverty. The model explains about 40% of the variation in family poverty across counties.









NoteExercise 2: Visualize with seaborn





Use lmplot or regplot to estimate and visualize a relationship between two variables. Customize the scatter transparency and line color.
Solution:

g = sb.lmplot(
    data=counties_df,
    x='Pct_Unemployed',
    y='Pct_Family_Poverty',
    aspect=2,
    scatter_kws={'alpha': .15, 's': 5},
    line_kws={'color': 'red'}
)










NoteExercise 3: Analyze residuals





Estimate a model, compute the residuals, and produce a residuals-vs-predictions plot and a histogram of residuals. Are the residuals independent, constant variance, normally distributed, and zero-centered?
Solution:

_model = stats.linregress(
    x=counties_df['Pct_College_Grad'].fillna(0),
    y=counties_df['Median_Income'].fillna(0)
)

_x = counties_df['Pct_College_Grad'].fillna(0)
_pred = _model.intercept + _model.slope * _x
_resid = _pred - counties_df['Median_Income'].fillna(0)

f, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
ax0.scatter(_pred, _resid, alpha=.2, s=5)
ax0.axhline(0, c='red', ls='--')
ax0.set_xlabel('Predicted')
ax0.set_ylabel('Residual')

_resid.hist(bins=30, ax=ax1)
ax1.axvline(0, c='red', ls='--')
ax1.set_xlabel('Residual')










NoteExercise 4: Multiple regression





Estimate a model predicting Pct_HS_Grad from both Pct_Born_Same_State and Median_Income using statsmodels. Compare the coefficients to the simple regressions. How does the \(R^2\) change?
Solution:

simple = smf.ols('Pct_HS_Grad ~ Pct_Born_Same_State', data=counties_df.fillna(0)).fit()
multiple = smf.ols('Pct_HS_Grad ~ Pct_Born_Same_State + Median_Income',
                    data=counties_df.fillna(0)).fit()

print(f"Simple R²: {simple.rsquared:.4f}")
print(f"Multiple R²: {multiple.rsquared:.4f}")
print(multiple.summary())

The \(R^2\) should increase because adding a relevant predictor always explains more variance. The coefficient for Pct_Born_Same_State may change because part of its original effect was confounded with income.

title: 13  Linear Models
text:
13.10 Looking Ahead
You have estimated, visualized, and interpreted linear regression models, examined residuals, and introduced multiple regression. In Chapter 14, you will confront the most important question in data analysis: does a statistical relationship reflect a causal mechanism, or is it a spurious correlation?

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.1 Setup
Throughout this book, you have loaded data, summarized it, visualized it, and modeled relationships. This final content chapter addresses the most consequential question in data analysis: does the pattern you found reflect a real causal mechanism, or is it an artifact of how the data was collected, analyzed, or presented?
This chapter introduces types of research validity, counterfactual reasoning, and quasi-experimental designs. We will use a concrete example — whether cannabis legalization in Colorado reduced opioid overdose deaths — to illustrate how these concepts shape the way you analyze data and communicate findings. The analysis draws on data from the CDC’s WONDER database and the research design frameworks from Freese and Kevern’s Handbook of Causal Analysis for Social Research and Reichardt’s entry on internal validity in the International Encyclopedia of the Social & Behavioral Sciences.
import numpy as np

import pandas as pd
idx = pd.IndexSlice
pd.options.display.max_columns = 200

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import ticker

import seaborn as sb
from scipy import stats

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.2 The Data
We use mortality data from the CDC’s WONDER database, which provides county-level death counts by cause from 1999 through 2020. We focus on drug/alcohol-induced causes using the “Unintentional” (X40–X44), “Suicide” (X60–X64), and “Undetermined” (Y10–Y14) ICD-10 codes to examine opioid overdose trends.

cod_df = pd.read_csv('wonder_cod.txt', sep='\t', dtype={'County Code': str})

# Extract the state from the "County" field (e.g., "Denver County, CO")
cod_df['State'] = cod_df['County'].str.split(', ').apply(lambda x: x[-1])

# Compute crude rate per 100k residents
cod_df['Rate'] = cod_df['Deaths'] / cod_df['Population'] * 100000

cod_df.head()

Not all counties are captured: the CDC suppresses small counts for privacy reasons, leaving only about 1,380 of the roughly 3,350 U.S. counties.

len(cod_df['County Code'].unique())


14.2.1 State-level rates
Pivot the data to compute annual state-level death rates per 100,000 residents.

deaths = pd.pivot_table(
    index='State', columns='Year', values='Deaths',
    aggfunc='sum', data=cod_df
)

population = pd.pivot_table(
    index='State', columns='Year', values='Population',
    aggfunc='sum', data=cod_df
)

state_rate_pivot = deaths / population * 100000
state_rate_pivot

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.3 The Question
Colorado legalized recreational cannabis on January 1, 2014. Some researchers have hypothesized that cannabis legalization reduces opioid overdose deaths because patients substitute cannabis for opioids in pain management. Let’s see what the data says.

legal_states = ['CO', 'WA']
nonlegal_states = [s for s in state_rate_pivot.index if s not in legal_states]


f, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6))

ax0.set_ylabel('Deaths per 100k')
ax1.set_ylabel('Deaths per 100k')

state_rate_pivot.loc[legal_states, :].T.plot(ax=ax0, zorder=2, lw=2)
state_rate_pivot.loc[nonlegal_states].T.plot(ax=ax0, c='k', linewidth=1,
                                              alpha=.25, zorder=1, legend=False)

state_rate_pivot.loc[legal_states, :].T.plot(ax=ax1, zorder=2, lw=2)
state_rate_pivot.loc[nonlegal_states].mean(0).plot(ax=ax1, c='k',
                                                    linewidth=5, zorder=1, label='Non-legal avg')
ax1.legend()


f, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 6))

state_rate_pivot.loc[legal_states, :].T.plot(ax=ax, zorder=2, lw=2)
state_rate_pivot.loc[nonlegal_states].mean(0).plot(ax=ax, c='k', linewidth=5, zorder=1)

ax.axvline(2014, color='r', ls='--', zorder=0)
f.tight_layout()

At first glance, Colorado’s rate appears to flatten around 2014 while the national average continues to climb. But is this evidence of a causal effect?

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.4 Types of Validity

14.4.1 Internal validity
Internal validity asks: did the treatment (cannabis legalization) cause the observed outcome (change in opioid deaths)? Threats to internal validity include confounding variables (other things that changed around 2014), selection bias (Colorado is not a random state), and maturation (trends that would have occurred anyway regardless of legalization).


14.4.2 External validity
External validity asks: would the same effect occur in other states? Even if legalization truly reduced opioid deaths in Colorado, the effect might not generalize to states with different demographics, healthcare systems, or drug markets.


14.4.3 Construct validity
Construct validity asks: are we measuring what we think we’re measuring? “Drug-induced deaths” is a broad category that includes many substances beyond opioids. Changes in coding practices, reporting requirements, or medical examiner staffing could affect the count without any real change in behavior.

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.5 Single-Group Pre/Post Design
The simplest design compares Colorado’s rates before and after legalization.

co_cod_df = cod_df[(cod_df['State'] == 'CO') & (cod_df['Year'] >= 2008)]

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
sb.boxplot(x='Year', y='Rate', data=co_cod_df, ax=ax, color='green')
ax.axvline(5.5, color='r', ls='--', zorder=2, label='Legalization (2014)')
ax.axhline(
    co_cod_df.loc[co_cod_df['Year'] == 2014, 'Rate'].mean(),
    color='r', ls='--', zorder=2
)
ax.set_ylim((0, 50))
ax.legend()


co_before = co_cod_df.loc[co_cod_df['Year'].isin(range(2008, 2014)), 'Rate']
co_after = co_cod_df.loc[co_cod_df['Year'].isin(range(2015, 2021)), 'Rate']

print(f"Before mean: {co_before.mean():.1f}")
print(f"After mean: {co_after.mean():.1f}")
print(f"Difference: {co_after.mean() - co_before.mean():.1f}")

stats.ttest_ind(co_after, co_before, equal_var=False)







WarningThe fatal flaw of pre/post designs



Even if the difference is statistically significant, we cannot attribute it to legalization. Many other things changed between 2008–2013 and 2014–2020: the opioid crisis intensified nationally, fentanyl entered the drug supply, and Medicaid expansion occurred. Without a comparison group, we cannot distinguish the effect of legalization from the effect of time.

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.6 Multiple-Group Comparison Design
A stronger design compares Colorado to states that did not legalize cannabis but had similar pre-legalization death rates.

state_rate_pivot.loc['CO', 2008:2013].mean()


state_rate_pivot.loc[:, 2008:2013].mean(1).sort_values()

We identify comparison states with similar pre-2014 rates and track all three trajectories.

co_before_df = cod_df.loc[
    (cod_df['State'] == 'CO') & (cod_df['Year'] >= 2008) & (cod_df['Year'] < 2014), 'Rate'
]
co_after_df = cod_df.loc[
    (cod_df['State'] == 'CO') & (cod_df['Year'] > 2014), 'Rate'
]

mt_before_df = cod_df.loc[
    (cod_df['State'] == 'MT') & (cod_df['Year'] >= 2008) & (cod_df['Year'] < 2014), 'Rate'
]
mt_after_df = cod_df.loc[
    (cod_df['State'] == 'MT') & (cod_df['Year'] > 2014), 'Rate'
]

de_before_df = cod_df.loc[
    (cod_df['State'] == 'DE') & (cod_df['Year'] >= 2008) & (cod_df['Year'] < 2014), 'Rate'
]
de_after_df = cod_df.loc[
    (cod_df['State'] == 'DE') & (cod_df['Year'] > 2014), 'Rate'
]


f, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(12, 6))

state_rate_pivot.loc[['CO', 'MT', 'DE', 'NM'], :].T.plot(ax=ax, zorder=2, lw=3)
state_rate_pivot.loc[nonlegal_states].mean(0).plot(ax=ax, c='k', linewidth=5, zorder=1)

ax.axvline(2014, color='r', ls='--', zorder=0)
f.tight_layout()


14.6.1 Visualize the distributions

f, axs = plt.subplots(
    nrows=1, ncols=3, figsize=(12, 4),
    subplot_kw={'xlim': (0, 100), 'ylim': (-.005, .2), 'xlabel': 'Opioid deaths per 100k'},
    sharey=True
)

co_before_df.plot.kde(ax=axs[0], color='blue')
co_after_df.plot.kde(ax=axs[0], color='red')
axs[0].axvline(co_before_df.mean(), color='blue', lw=.5)
axs[0].axvline(co_after_df.mean(), color='red', lw=.5)
axs[0].set_title('Colorado')

mt_before_df.plot.kde(ax=axs[1], color='blue')
mt_after_df.plot.kde(ax=axs[1], color='red')
axs[1].axvline(mt_before_df.mean(), color='blue', lw=.5)
axs[1].axvline(mt_after_df.mean(), color='red', lw=.5)
axs[1].set_title('Montana')

de_before_df.plot.kde(ax=axs[2], color='blue')
de_after_df.plot.kde(ax=axs[2], color='red')
axs[2].axvline(de_before_df.mean(), color='blue', lw=.5)
axs[2].axvline(de_after_df.mean(), color='red', lw=.5)
axs[2].set_title('Delaware')

f.tight_layout()



14.6.2 Compare changes across states

for name, before, after in [('Colorado', co_before_df, co_after_df),
                             ('Montana', mt_before_df, mt_after_df),
                             ('Delaware', de_before_df, de_after_df)]:
    diff = after.mean() - before.mean()
    t, p = stats.ttest_ind(after, before, equal_var=False)
    print(f"{name}: before {before.mean():.1f}, after {after.mean():.1f}, "
          f"diff {diff:+.1f}, t={t:.2f}, p={p:.4f}")

Montana’s increase is non-significant (p > 0.05) while Delaware’s increase is highly significant (p < 0.001), reflecting the fentanyl crisis that hit East Coast states particularly hard.

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.7 Counterfactual Reasoning
The core challenge of causal inference is that we can never observe the counterfactual — what would have happened to Colorado if it had not legalized cannabis. The comparison group serves as a proxy for this counterfactual, but it is only as good as the match between the treatment and comparison groups.
This is why randomized controlled experiments are the gold standard: random assignment ensures comparability on all characteristics, observed and unobserved. Observational studies like ours can never fully eliminate confounding.

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.8 Difference-in-Differences
A common quasi-experimental approach is difference-in-differences (DiD): compute the before-to-after change in the treatment group and subtract the before-to-after change in the comparison group.

co_change = co_after_df.mean() - co_before_df.mean()
mt_change = mt_after_df.mean() - mt_before_df.mean()

did = co_change - mt_change

print(f"Colorado change: {co_change:+.1f}")
print(f"Montana change: {mt_change:+.1f}")
print(f"Difference-in-differences: {did:+.1f}")

A negative DiD estimate would suggest that Colorado’s rate increased less (or decreased more) than Montana’s, consistent with a protective effect. But even this design relies on the parallel trends assumption — that Colorado and Montana would have followed the same trajectory in the absence of legalization.

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.9 Communicating Causal Claims
When you present findings from observational data, your language must reflect the limitations of your design.
Don’t write: “Cannabis legalization reduced opioid deaths in Colorado.”
Write instead: “Colorado’s drug-induced death rate increased less than comparable non-legalization states between 2008 and 2020, though this cross-state comparison cannot definitively attribute the difference to legalization.”
This is not hedging for its own sake — it is honest communication about what the evidence can and cannot support. As discussed in Chapter 11, your credibility depends on being clear about the boundaries of your knowledge.






NoteExercise 1: Identify threats to validity





List three specific threats to internal validity for the cannabis-opioid analysis. For each, explain how it could bias the results in either direction.
Solution:

Confounding with Medicaid expansion: Colorado expanded Medicaid in 2014, the same year as legalization. Increased access to addiction treatment could reduce overdose deaths independently of cannabis access, inflating the apparent protective effect.
Changes in drug supply: The rise of fentanyl accelerated after 2014, disproportionately affecting some states. If fentanyl reached Colorado later than comparison states, the apparent protective effect would be overstated.
Reporting changes: Improvements in medical examiner toxicology testing could change how deaths are classified. A state that began testing for more substances might reclassify deaths from “drug-induced” to more specific categories, artificially reducing the count.










NoteExercise 2: Choose better comparison states





Using the state_rate_pivot data, identify the three states whose pre-2014 death rates most closely match Colorado’s. Plot their trajectories alongside Colorado’s. Does the visual evidence support or undermine the legalization hypothesis?
Solution:

co_pre = state_rate_pivot.loc['CO', 2008:2013].mean()
distances = (state_rate_pivot.loc[:, 2008:2013].mean(1) - co_pre).abs()
best_matches = distances.drop(['CO', 'WA']).sort_values().head(3).index.tolist()

print(f"Best matches: {best_matches}")

f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
state_rate_pivot.loc[['CO'] + best_matches, :].T.plot(ax=ax, linewidth=2)
ax.axvline(2014, color='black', linestyle=':')
ax.set_ylabel('Deaths per 100,000')
ax.set_title('Colorado vs. Best-Matched Comparison States')
ax.legend()










NoteExercise 3: Compute a difference-in-differences





Using the comparison states you identified in Exercise 2, compute the difference-in-differences estimate for each pair (Colorado vs. each comparison state). Are the estimates consistent across comparisons?
Solution:

co_change = co_after_df.mean() - co_before_df.mean()

for state in best_matches:
    before = cod_df.loc[
        (cod_df['State'] == state) & (cod_df['Year'] >= 2008) & (cod_df['Year'] < 2014), 'Rate'
    ]
    after = cod_df.loc[
        (cod_df['State'] == state) & (cod_df['Year'] > 2014), 'Rate'
    ]
    change = after.mean() - before.mean()
    did = co_change - change
    print(f"CO vs {state}: DiD = {did:+.1f}")

If the DiD estimates are consistent (all negative, or all positive, or all near zero), the finding is more robust. If they vary widely, the result is sensitive to the choice of comparison group — a red flag.









NoteExercise 4: Write a data brief





Write a 200-word data brief for a state legislator considering cannabis legalization. Include one visualization, one key statistic, and explicit acknowledgment of what the evidence can and cannot tell us.
Solution:
This is an open-ended exercise. A strong response leads with the finding, provides one clear chart, cites the DiD estimate, and concludes with caveats about confounding and the limits of cross-state comparison. The brief should avoid claiming causation while being clear about the direction and magnitude of the observed association.

title: 14  Validity and Causal Reasoning
text:
14.10 Looking Back
This chapter concludes the content of the book. You have traveled from the mechanics of loading a CSV file to the philosophical foundations of causal inference. Along the way, you developed skills in data shaping, visualization, temporal analysis, statistical modeling, and — most importantly — the communication of findings in a way that is honest, clear, and appropriate for your audience.
These skills are not separate competencies to be checked off a list. They form an integrated practice of thinking carefully about data and the stories it can and cannot tell. Every chart you make embeds assumptions about what matters and what does not. Every regression coefficient you report implies a theory about how the world works. Every audience you address deserves an honest accounting of what you know and what remains uncertain.
The best data analysts are not the ones who produce the most sophisticated models or the most polished charts. They are the ones who ask the right questions, acknowledge the right limitations, and communicate with clarity and integrity.

title: 15  Datasets
text:
15.1 Part I: Shaping Data
This appendix describes each dataset used in the book. All files are available in the companion GitHub repository at https://github.com/cuinfoscience/Information-Exposition-Book/. Datasets are organized by the part and chapter in which they first appear.

title: 15  Datasets
text:
ACS 2019 Demographic Profiles (ACS2019_DP05_state.csv)
Source: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates, Table DP05 (Demographic and Housing Estimates). Retrieved from https://data.census.gov.
Description: State-level demographic estimates including population by age, sex, race, and Hispanic origin. Each row is a U.S. state or territory. Columns follow the Census naming convention with prefixes for “Estimate,” “Margin of Error,” and “Percent.” Multiple codes represent different types of missing data: (X) for not applicable, ***** for controlled estimates, - for too few observations, + for upper-bound medians, and *** for open-ended distribution boundaries.
Used in: Chapter 1


CDC Weekly Mortality Data (CDC_deaths.csv)
Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, National Center for Health Statistics. Weekly counts of deaths by cause and state, derived from two CDC datasets: 2014–2019 and 2020–2023.
Description: Weekly death counts for each of 52 U.S. states and territories (50 states plus D.C. and Puerto Rico) from 2014 through 2023, broken down by cause of death including heart disease, cancer, diabetes, septicemia, influenza and pneumonia, chronic lower respiratory diseases, cerebrovascular diseases, Alzheimer disease, and COVID-19. The “Week date” column contains the ending date of each epidemiological week. The 2014 and 2020 data include a 53rd epidemiological week due to calendar alignment. Missing values have been filled with zeros.
Used in: Chapter 2, Chapter 5, Chapter 4, Chapter 11


Lahman Baseball Database (Lahman/)
Source: Sean Lahman’s Baseball Database, http://seanlahman.com/.
Description: A collection of CSV files covering Major League Baseball statistics. The book uses several tables from this database. Batting.csv contains season-level batting statistics for every player, with each row being a player-season-team combination identified by playerID, yearID, and teamID. People.csv contains biographical information for every player and manager including birth date, birth country, and death date. HallOfFame.csv tracks Hall of Fame balloting. Salaries.csv records player salary data. CollegePlaying.csv and Schools.csv link players to their college backgrounds.
Used in: Chapter 3


Retrosheet Game Logs (Retrosheet/GL*.TXT)
Source: Retrosheet, http://www.retrosheet.org/gamelogs/index.html. Column documentation: https://www.retrosheet.org/gamelogs/glfields.txt.
Description: 151 files, one per MLB season from 1871 through 2021. Each row is a single game with 161 columns recording date (column 1), visiting team (column 4), home team (column 7), visiting team score (column 10), home team score (column 11), game day/night indicator (column 13), attendance (column 18), and detailed box score data for pitchers, batters, and fielders. Files have no header row; columns are documented starting from 1 (not 0) in the Retrosheet documentation.
Used in: Chapter 3


Denver International Airport Passenger Traffic (passengers.csv)
Source: Denver International Airport Statistics Office, supplemented by Bureau of Transportation Statistics data.
Description: Monthly passenger counts by airline at Denver International Airport from January 1995 through late 2021. Columns represent individual airlines (American, Delta, Frontier, Other, Southwest, United, United Express) plus a “Grand” total and a “Cargo” column. The airport opened in February 1995. Data was originally provided as an Excel file with separate sheets for passengers and flights, from which the passenger data was extracted and cleaned.
Used in: Chapter 4, Chapter 5, Chapter 8, Chapter 10


Bureau of Transportation Statistics Passengers (transtats_passengers.xlsx)
Source: U.S. Department of Transportation, Bureau of Transportation Statistics, https://www.transtats.bts.gov/Data_Elements.aspx?Data=1 (select “Denver, CO: Denver International”).
Description: Monthly passenger enplanement data for Denver International Airport. Used to validate the DIA-provided statistics. Contains Year, Month, and TOTAL columns, with annual total rows that must be filtered out.
Used in: Chapter 3, Chapter 4

title: 15  Datasets
text:
15.2 Part II: Distributions

Boulder and Broomfield Weather (boulder_weather.csv, broomfield_weather.csv)
Source: NOAA Global Historical Climatology Network Daily (GHCN-D).
Description: Daily weather observations from stations near Boulder and Broomfield, Colorado, covering approximately 2010–2021. Pipe-delimited (|) files with columns for date (DATE), temperature (TEMP), maximum temperature (MAX), minimum temperature (MIN), precipitation (PRCP), wind speed (WDSP), and other measurements. Values are in U.S. customary units (Fahrenheit, inches, miles per hour). The two datasets have slightly different date ranges, so an inner join on DATE is necessary to compare the same days.
Used in: Chapter 6


Colorado County COVID-19 Data (co_county_covid.csv)
Source: Derived from Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment reports.
Description: Monthly COVID-19 case and death counts by Colorado county, covering 2020–2022. Each row is a county-month observation with columns for county name, month, case count (Cases), and death count (Deaths). The data exhibits strong right skew: most county-months have few cases while a small number of county-months (primarily Denver and its suburbs) have thousands.
Used in: Chapter 6


House of Representatives Disbursement Data (2021Q3-house-disburse-detail.csv)
Source: ProPublica, https://projects.propublica.org/represent/expenditures.
Description: Detailed spending records for every U.S. House office in the third quarter of 2021. Columns include the office’s BIOGUIDE_ID (a standardized identifier for every senator and representative since 1790), spending category (CATEGORY), payee, amount (AMOUNT), and sort sequence (SORT SEQUENCE). The SORT SEQUENCE column includes “DETAIL” rows for individual transactions and “SUBTOTAL” rows for aggregations — filtering to only “DETAIL” rows avoids double-counting. Rows with null BIOGUIDE_ID represent non-member offices (committees, officers, administrators).
Used in: Chapter 7


House Members Data (house_members.csv)
Source: Derived from the ProPublica Congress API, VoteView DW-NOMINATE scores, and MIT Election Data + Science Lab house election returns. See the Week 07 notebook appendix for construction details.
Description: Biographical and political data for members of the U.S. House of Representatives including party caucus (D, R, or I), state, full state name (state-fullname), office building, prior congressional service (prior-congress), DW-NOMINATE ideology scores (nominate_dim1, nominate_dim2), and election-related data. The prior-congress field indicates whether the member is an incumbent (nonzero) or a newcomer (zero).
Used in: Chapter 7


Country Military Data (country_military.csv)
Source: World Bank, World Development Indicators, https://databank.worldbank.org/source/world-development-indicators.
Description: Cross-national panel data on military expenditures (as percentage of GDP and of government expenditure), armed forces personnel (total and as percentage of labor force), arms imports and exports (SIPRI values), labor force, and GDP for 266 countries from 2010–2020. Missing values are coded as .. in the raw file and should be passed to read_csv via the na_values parameter.
Used in: Chapter 7


Regional Classifications (regional_classifications.csv)
Source: United Nations Statistics Division and World Bank regional classification lists.
Description: Country-level regional classification data with columns for Country Name, Iso3 code, UN Regions, UN Sub-regions, World Bank Regions, and World Bank Income Groups. Used to add categorical grouping variables to the country military data for catplot visualizations.
Used in: Chapter 7

title: 15  Datasets
text:
15.3 Part III: Trends

S&P 500 Historical Data (sp500.csv)
Source: Retrieved via the yfinance Python library using the ^GSPC ticker symbol.
Description: Daily market data for the S&P 500 index from January 2010 to October 2023. Columns include Date, Open, High, Low, Close, Adj Close (adjusted for dividends and splits), and Volume. The data contains trading days only — weekends and holidays have no entries, which creates gaps in a daily time series that must be handled via reindexing (see Chapter 9).
Used in: Chapter 9, Chapter 10


NYC Taxi Pickups (nyc_pickups.csv)
Source: New York City Taxi and Limousine Commission, https://www1.nyc.gov/site/tlc/about/tlc-trip-record-data.page. Derived from March 2020 yellow cab and for-hire vehicle (FHV) trip data.
Description: Minute-level pickup counts for yellow cabs and rideshare services (Uber, Lyft) in New York City for March 2020. Each row is a timestamp (one per minute) with corresponding pickup counts for yellowcab and rideshare. The data captures the dramatic impact of COVID-19 lockdowns on ride activity and exhibits strong hourly (circadian), daily (weekday/weekend), and within-month patterns.
Used in: Chapter 10


Berkeley Earth Surface Temperature (avg_temp_1880_2024.csv)
Source: Berkeley Earth, http://berkeleyearth.org/data/. “Daily Land” average temperature estimates.
Description: Daily average land surface temperature estimates from 1880 to 2024. Each row is a date with the corresponding average temperature in degrees Celsius, computed as an anomaly from the 1951–1980 average of 8.59°C added back to produce an absolute temperature. This long-duration, high-frequency dataset (50,000+ observations) is ideal for illustrating resampling, rolling averages, seasonal decomposition, and long-term trend detection.
Used in: Chapter 10, Chapter 11


Wikipedia Pageview Data
Source: Retrieved via the wikifunctions library (source) from the Wikimedia Pageviews API.
Description: Daily pageview counts for Wikipedia articles. The book uses the “University of Colorado Boulder” article as the primary example. The data exhibits strong weekly (lower on weekends) and seasonal (academic calendar) patterns. The wikifunctions library is not available via conda or pip and must be placed in the same directory as the notebook.
Used in: Chapter 10

title: 15  Datasets
text:
15.4 Part IV: Relationships

U.S. Counties Data (us_counties.csv)
Source: Compiled from multiple sources: U.S. Census Bureau ACS 5-Year Estimates (DP02 social characteristics, DP03 economic characteristics, DP05 demographic characteristics), MIT Election Data + Science Lab (county-level presidential returns 2000–2020), and The New York Times COVID-19 county-level tracking project.
Description: A merged cross-sectional dataset of approximately 3,200 U.S. counties. Variables include demographic characteristics (Population, Pct_Under_18, Pct_Over_65, Pct_Female, Pct_White, Pct_Hispanic, Pct_Born_Same_State), economic characteristics (Median_Income, Pct_Unemployed, Pct_Family_Poverty, Pct_Broadband, Pct_Assistance), education (Pct_HS_Grad, Pct_College_Grad), language (Pct_English_Only), household structure (Pct_Single_Female), 2016 and 2020 presidential vote shares (trump_2016, trump_2020, 16_20_shift), and cumulative COVID-19 case and death counts (covid_cases, covid_deaths) with per-capita derivatives. The first column is the FIPS code — a five-digit county identifier that should be read as a string (not an integer) to preserve leading zeros. The - character is used for missing values and should be passed to na_values in read_csv.
Used in: Chapter 12, Chapter 13


CDC WONDER Mortality Data (wonder_cod.txt)
Source: CDC WONDER database, https://wonder.cdc.gov/. Underlying Cause of Death query system.
Description: County-level drug/alcohol-induced death counts and population from 1999 through 2020, extracted using the “Drug/Alcohol Induced Causes” option and filtered to three ICD-10 cause groups: Unintentional (X40–X44), Suicide (X60–X64), and Undetermined (Y10–Y14). Tab-delimited with columns for County, County Code, Year, Deaths, and Population. Not all counties are represented (only approximately 1,380 of 3,350) because the CDC suppresses counts of fewer than 10 for privacy. The “State” column must be derived by splitting the “County” field on the comma delimiter. A crude death rate per 100,000 should be computed as Deaths / Population * 100000.
Used in: Chapter 14

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.1 Installing Anaconda
This appendix provides instructions for setting up the Python environment needed to run the code in this book. All code was developed and tested with the Anaconda distribution of Python 3.10 running in Jupyter Notebook.
We recommend the Anaconda distribution of Python, which bundles Python with the most commonly used data science libraries. Download the installer for your operating system from https://www.anaconda.com/download and follow the installation instructions. Choose Python 3.10 or above.
Anaconda includes all of the core libraries used in this book: numpy, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, scipy, scikit-learn, and statsmodels. It also includes Jupyter Notebook and JupyterLab, the interactive computing environments in which this book’s code is designed to run.

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.2 Verifying Your Installation
After installing Anaconda, open a terminal (macOS/Linux) or Anaconda Prompt (Windows) and verify your Python version and key library installations.
python --version
You should see Python 3.10 or higher. Then verify the key libraries.
python -c "import pandas; print('pandas', pandas.__version__)"
python -c "import matplotlib; print('matplotlib', matplotlib.__version__)"
python -c "import seaborn; print('seaborn', seaborn.__version__)"
python -c "import scipy; print('scipy', scipy.__version__)"
python -c "import sklearn; print('scikit-learn', sklearn.__version__)"
python -c "import statsmodels; print('statsmodels', statsmodels.__version__)"

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.3 Launching Jupyter
To start a Jupyter Notebook server, navigate to the directory containing the book’s data files and run one of the following commands.
jupyter notebook
jupyter lab
This will open a browser window where you can create new notebooks or open existing .ipynb files. The classic Notebook interface (jupyter notebook) is simpler and more widely documented. The JupyterLab interface (jupyter lab) offers a more modern multi-pane layout with a file browser, terminal, and multiple notebook tabs.

text:

TipImport conventions matter



The aliases shown above (np, pd, plt, sb) are widely used conventions in the Python data science community. Using them makes your code consistent with tutorials, documentation, and Stack Overflow answers. Note that some resources import seaborn as sns rather than sb — both work, and the choice is a matter of preference.



16.4.1 Optional Libraries
Two additional libraries appear in specific chapters but are not required for the majority of the book.
wikifunctions is a custom library for retrieving data from Wikipedia APIs, used in Chapter 10. It is not available via conda or pip and must be downloaded from https://github.com/brianckeegan/wikifunctions/ and placed in the same directory as the notebook.
ipympl enables interactive matplotlib widgets in Jupyter Notebook and is used in Chapter 13 for interactive 3D regression visualization. Install it with conda install ipympl -c conda-forge if you want to use the %matplotlib widget magic command.

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.5 Installing Additional Packages
If any library is missing, install it using conda (preferred for compatibility) or pip.
conda install seaborn
pip install seaborn
For pip installations in environments managed by Anaconda, you may need the --break-system-packages flag on newer Python versions.

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.6 Common Setup Block
Most chapters begin with a standard setup block. You can copy this into the first cell of any notebook.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100

%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sb

The %matplotlib inline cell magic ensures that plots render directly in the notebook rather than opening in a separate window. The pd.options.display.max_columns setting prevents pandas from truncating wide DataFrames when you call .head() or display a DataFrame.

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.7 Getting the Data
Clone the book’s repository to download all datasets.
git clone https://github.com/cuinfoscience/Information-Exposition-Book.git
cd Information-Exposition-Book
See Chapter 15 for a description of each dataset file, including its source, format, and any special loading parameters required (such as sep='|' for pipe-delimited files or na_values='..' for World Bank data).

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.8 Working Directory
Many code cells in this book load data using relative file paths like pd.read_csv('sp500.csv'). This assumes that the notebook’s working directory is the same directory that contains the data files. You can check and change the working directory with the following code.

import os
print(os.getcwd())


# Uncomment and modify to change directory
# os.chdir('/path/to/data/directory')

If you organize your data into subdirectories (e.g., data/Retrosheet/, data/Lahman/), you will need to adjust the file paths in the code accordingly. The os.listdir() function, introduced in Chapter 3, can help you explore directory contents.

title: 16  Python Environment Setup
text:
16.9 Troubleshooting
ModuleNotFoundError when importing a library. The library is not installed in your current Python environment. Use conda install or pip install to add it. If you have multiple Python environments, make sure you are installing into the correct one.
Plots do not appear in the notebook. Ensure that %matplotlib inline appears in a cell that has been executed before any plotting code. If you restart the kernel, you need to re-execute the setup cell.
FileNotFoundError when calling read_csv. The notebook’s working directory does not match the location of the data files. Use os.getcwd() to check where the notebook thinks it is, and either move the data or change the working directory.
Pandas displays ... instead of all columns. The DataFrame has more columns than the display limit. Increase the limit with pd.options.display.max_columns = 200 or use .head().T to transpose the display.
SettingWithCopyWarning. This warning indicates that you are modifying a “view” of a DataFrame rather than an independent copy. To silence it and ensure correct behavior, use .copy() when creating a filtered DataFrame, as described in Chapter 12.
Memory errors with large datasets. If you are working with the full Retrosheet game log concatenation (150,000+ rows × 161 columns) on a machine with limited RAM, consider using the nrows parameter in read_csv to work with a subset, or use dtype to specify memory-efficient types for columns you don’t need at full precision.
